Established biodiversity management goal: "aggregate OGMAs within/across BEC variants to implement biodiversity management provisions like connectivity"; subsequent strategy: "delineate OGMAs that are as large and contiguous as possible, while ensuring that they contain a wide range of sites and habitat types."

Connectivity is quantified using the Biodiversity Emphasis Option (BEO) ranking criteria for the Squamish Forest District; non-PAS connectivity is very low (0 out a 0-3 scale) and PAS associated connectivity is low (1 out of a 0-3 scale) (see appendix 32).

Plan.Type: Landscape Unit Plan for OGMAs
Plan.Name: Landscape Unit Plan for Old-Growth Management Areas
Connectivity.Information:
The plan emphasizes strategically placeing OGMAs within/adjacent to wildlife management areas in order to create larger patches that improve connectivity between habitat favourable to wildlife - spotted owl, marbled murrelet, deer, moose, goshawk, grizzly bear, and mountain goat referenced.

Plan aims to position OGMA adjacent to ungulate winter ranges to increase their habitat patch size - "For species such as deer, which are particularly susceptible to mortality in winter, connecting or aggregating OGMAs with areas designated for deer winter range effectively augments deer habitat, in addition to benefiting general biodiversity."

Connectivity is not defined or supported with citations.
OGWG_Connectivity.LegalTools.Methods.Imp: Land Act, Land Use Objectives Regulation; Oil & Gas Activities Act, Environmental Protection and Management Regulation.
Corridor.Information:
Plan states that "effective Landscape Unit planning retains (or recruits) a series of old forest habitat patches across probable movement corridors to allow wildlife dispersal."

"[Wildlife habitat areas] located along the corridor of the Squamish River and adjacent to Garibaldi Provincial Park are legally established under [the Government Actions Regulation]."

The plan states that Wildlife Management Areas may also provide important buffer zones, habitat corridors or linkages between core protected areas. Such linkages may be essential to enable movement of species during seasonal migrations or in response to short-term ecological variations or longer-term climate changes - 

Corridors are not defined.
Corridor.References:
Government Actions Regulation (GAR) - establishes legal protections for wildlife habitat within corridors.

BC Wildlife Act - the plan states that the primary designation tool for conservation lands is the "Wildlife Management Area" designation under section 4 of this act; plan states that these areas may provide habitat corridors.

Plan.Type: Landscape Unit Plan
Plan.Name: Bunster Landscape Unit Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan quantifies connectivity values of the landscape unit using the Biodiversity Emphasis Option (BEO) ranking criteria for the Sunshine Coast Forest District - "Higher BEO ranking scores will be given under this criteria to those LUs that have old seral stage forest in large contiguous stands, or in areas where harvesting has not disrupted natural connectivity due to natural patchy non-contiguous patterns" (see appendix 33 and 34).

Plan states that "opportunities to maintain connectivity through second growth management adjacent to natural riparian linkages/ gully systems will improve over time as the forests mature and OGMA distribution is revised."

Established management strategy: deineate OGMAs to be as large/continguous as possible, and delineate OG recruitment OGMAs within stands to contribute to connectivity between OG OGMAs; associated management goals: 1) maintain a wide range of ecosystem types and species composition within the CWHxm1, 2) maximize interior forest condition within OGMAs, and 3) include areas of multiple resource values within OGMAs where compatible with biodiversity management.

It is stated that "connectivity is not a primary objective of the Bunster LU plan."

Bunster landscape unit summary of public input:  "Landscape Unit Planning Guide connectivity provisions should not be lower priority, and should be incorporated at this time."

Connectivity is not defined.
Connectivity.References:
Landscape Unit Planning Guide - plan states that "consistent with the LU Planning Guide, connectivity is not a primary objective of the LU Plan."

Forest Practices Code Higher Level Plans: Policy and Procedures (FPC HLPPP) - plan refers to a direction regarding protected areas from the FPC HLPPP that "higher BEOs should be assigned for LUs adjacent to protected areas"; plan follows this direction because the Squamish Forest District has received more protected area forest ecosystem representation than some other Districts making ecosystem representation a lesser priority, and the location of some of the protected areas do offer easily achievable opportunities for connectivity.
Connectivity.Ref.Diag2: Forest Practices Code Higher Level Plans: Policy and Procedures (FPC HLPPP)

Connectivity.Information:

Young forest stands with OGMA charactersitcs have been included in plan to ehance future connectivity efforts.

The plan recognizes OGMAs #3, 7, 9, and 10 for providing connectivity between Skookumchuk Park and Brown Lake.

The plan recognizes OGMAs #41 and 48 for providing cross slope connectivity.

The plan recognizes OGMA #57 for providing connectivity between Agamemnon Channel and Sakinaw Lake.

The plan recognizes OGMAs #110 and 111 for providing connectivity with the existing provincial park.

Connectivity is not defined.

Plan.Type: Sustainable Resource Management Plan (SRMP)
Plan.Name: Bute East Landscape Unit: Sustainable Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
This plan emphasizes the alignment of OGMAs with other protected areas (e.g., UWR, WHA, inoperable areas) to improve connectivity, reduce fragmentation, and support ecosystem management at the landscape scale.

This plan states that riparian reserve zones and wildlife tree patches at the stand level will enhance connectivity, supporting broader landscape-level biodiversity objectives.

The plan focuses on strategically placing OGMAs in or near areas already restricted from harvesting, resulting in larger forest patches. These larger patches improve connectivity between adjacent habitats, facilitating movement and gene flow for species.

Supports the expansion of riparian OGMAs to connect valley bottoms with upslope areas, thereby enhancing connectivity across elevation gradients despite limited opportunities due to historical disturbances; also maintains OGMA distribution to prioritize inter-watershed connectivity, though natural barriers like alpine terrain may limit dispersal for less mobile species.

Connectivity is not defined.
Corridor.Information:
The plan cites that it is supporting movement corridors to aid wildlife dispersal and gene flow through the plan's OGMA placement strategy to maintain connectivity between adjacent old forest patches.

No specific management strategies/objectives related to ecological corridors otherwise.
WildlifeMovement.Information: The plan states that "certain wildlife species are particularly susceptible to mortality in winter and connecting or aggregating OGMAs may help facilitate movement to more suitable habitats."
Network.Information: Ecological networks are not referenced or managed for in this plan.

Plan.Type: Sustainable Resource Management Plan (SRMP)
Plan.Name: Howe Landscape Unit: Sustainable Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan states that OGMAs were strategically delineated to create contiguous areas across biogeoclimatic subzones, improving connectivity across ecological boundaries and enhancing landscape-level biodiversity.

Riparian OGMAs were expanded to include adjacent upland forests when ecologically viable, enhancing connectivity from water-adjacent ecosystems to upland habitats.

While fragmented landscapes limited patch size options, large, contiguous OGMAs were prioritized over smaller, isolated ones to preserve interior habitat conditions, enhancing connectivity within and across forest patches.

OGMAs were intentionally distributed throughout the landscape unit to establish connectivity at a landscape scale, ensuring biodiversity continuity across regions.

Incrementally retained forests, including sensitive terrain, riparian areas, and wetlands, were included to improve connectivity, enlarge OGMA patch size, reduce edge effects, and support interior forest conditions, even though these areas lack formal OGMA protection.

Connectivity is not defined.
Connectivity.References: The Landscape Unit Planning Guide (LUPG) - provides direction for selecting OGMA candiadate stands which maximize biodiversity, and connectivity is managed thorugh OGMA placement.
Corridor.Information:
The plan states that "narrow and isolated riparian corridors were not considered viable OGMA candidates due to their limited value at the landscape scale."

No specific management strategies/objectives related to ecological corridors.
Network.Information: Ecological networks are not referenced or managed for in this plan.

Plan.Type: Landscape Unit Plan for OGMAs
Plan.Name: Homfray Landscape Unit Plan for Old Growth Management Areas
Connectivity.Information:
The plan states that connectivity was considered during the plan area's OGMA delineation  - and opportunties to recruit larger patches to provide for forest interior conditions were favoured over smaller patches.

"Although connectivity is not a primary objective in biodiversity planning..., it was considered when delineating OGMAs in the Salmon Inlet LU. Connectivity opportunities from lower to higher elevations may only exist in a few areas due to the contiguous lower elevation disturbance history. Also, the inaccessible and higher elevation areas have a higher proportion of old growth. With the inclusion of the younger aged stands in the constrained forest, such as within riparian reserve zones, the connectivity between lower and higher elevation OGMAs will be maintained and improve over time."

Plan states that old growth targets in the CWH vm2 and MH mm1 were achieved with OGMAs aligned contiguously to those in the CWH vm1, enhancing connectivity across the Landscape Unit.

The plan emphasizes that it aims to overlap OGMAs with Ungulate Winter Ranges (UWRs) and wildlife habitat areas for species like marbled murrelets to facilitate connectivity where ecologically possible.

Connectivity is not defined.

[tend] to overwinter on some of the more challenging slopes found between the Sockeye Creek and Phelix Creek drainages. Field reconnaissance has recorded from 30 to 40 goats observed in the headwaters of Sockeye Creek."

Plan.Type: First Nation Developed Forest Resource Plan
Plan.Name: Tla'amin Nation Forest Resource Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan defines that forest connectivity "plays an important role in the survival and adaptation of species, the resilience of ecosystems, and the ability of forests to adapt to a changing climate. Connected forests allow animals, plants, and other species to move freely across landscapes, which is essential for finding food, mates, suitable conditions as seasons or climates change, and for avoiding predators. Forest connectivity also improves ecosystem resilience, as forests can more easily withstand and recover from disturbances and changing climates than highly fragmented ones."

Plan recognizes that "man-made barriers such as paved roads, communities and BC Hydro lines were considered to break forest connectivity"

Includes projections for connectivity - assesses forest connectivity across various regions and was evaluated based on stands 60 years and older, with forecasts indicating a 270% increase in connectivity for stands 141+ years old. However, overall connectivity is expected to decrease slightly due to a reduction in connectivity for 60-140 year old stands, as many of these mature into older age classes or are harvested..

The plan includes a chart and map (figures 9 and 10) showcasing the projected increase in forest connectivity among stands aged 61-140 and 141+ years.

A listed outcomes for wildlife habitat type representation: species for which patch size and connectivity are considered important.

The plan states that maintaining a diversity of forest habitat types is an important landscape level approach to managing wildlife habitat; it includes an approach that was based on a species accounting system that assigns species into six groups with similar habitat requirements - one being species for which patch size and connectivity are considered important.

No supporting citations for connectivity.
OGWG_Connectivity.LegalTools.Methods.Imp: None yet, FSPs and FLP will be legal tools used to implement the FRP.
OGWG_Connectivity.Additional.Info: The Tla'amin FRP has been approved by Hegus and Council, but the management strategies will not become legal/integrated into practice until they are adopted in licensee's FSPs and the Sunshine Coast FLP.  The Tla'amin FSP for their community forest (K3P) and NRFL (A98255) has been submitted to the District for review (current FSP expires in Feb 2025). This FSP (though not yet approved) includes a table of FRP measures that are to apply to operations under the FSP - FRP measures in Part 2, Division 2, 4(1)(2)(3)(4) address connectivity.
Corridor.Information:
Wildlife/ecological corridors not mentioned.

No management objectives or strategies referring to ecological corridors.
Network.Information:
Plan defines conservation networks:  "the areas shown on the map attached as Schedule C, for the purpose of carbon storage, while managing cultural sites and practices, protecting wildlife habitat, preserving ecosystem health, conserving our water, and creating a resilient landscape."

The plan includes that conservation networks are protected from road construction and industrial harvesting and include legally established reserves for critical species; modifications can occur with Tlaamin Nation approval, allowing for cultural/recreational use, provided they align with conservation objectives - annual documentation of modifications is required.

Several strategies and measures concerning conservation networks (see appendix 103).
OGWG_Network.LegalTools.Methods.Implemen: None yet, FSPs and FLP will be legal tools used to implement the FRP.

The Tla'amin FSP for their community forest (K3P) and NRFL (A98255) has been submitted to the District for review (current FSP expires in Feb 2025). This FSP (though not yet approved) includes a table of FRP measures that are to apply to operations under the FSP - FRP measures in Part 2, Division 1, 3(1)(2)(3) address ecosystem networks.

Plan.Type: Watershed Action Plan
Plan.Name: Coquitlam-Buntzen Watershed Action Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan includes the enhancement of habitat connectivity as a habitat-based management action.

Habitat-based management actions are referenced under the priority actions of: 1) Assess success of habitat-based actions, 2) Land securement in association with partner organizations to address fish and wildlife management objectives or to support habitat-based actions, 3) conduct a limiting factors analysis to assess habitat status, capacity, and the cost-benefit of habitat-based actions, and 4) develop a comprehensive restoration and protection plan focused on habitat-based actions.

Regarding wetland and riparian areas, connectivity and distribution of habitat is listed as a limiting factor - "connectivity among riparian and wetland habitats, and between these habitats and other habitats and features, are important for dispersal of plants and animals and for seasonal movements of some species. Wetland and riparian habitats that are isolated will likely have decreased diversity compared to those which experience a healthy connectivity between areas. Distribution is therefore related not only to the extent of healthy riparian and wetland habitats, but also to adjacent land uses"

Regarding upland and dryland areas, habitat connectivity is listed as a limiting factor that needs to be further addresses - "habitat loss and road development have resulted in lost connectivity between habitats, which alter wildlife movement."

In the plan's action table that identifies priority actions to conserve and enhance fish and wildlif e impacted by BC Hydro dams in this watershed, there is a priority action to "develop a current habitat assessment map for priority fish and/or wildlife species in the coquitlam watershed" which will include connectivity corridors.

Connectivity is not defined or supported with citations.
Corridor.Information: Corridors are only referenced and managed for as connectivity corridors (see connectivity column).
WildlifeMovement.Information:
The plan includes migration barriers in hydro-related impacts - "Coquitlam Dam blocked passage of anadromous stocks. The tailrace discharges of the powerhouses into Indian Arm attract Chinook salmon. The number of fish affected and the impacts of the delay on reproductive success are unknown. There is reduced fish access between the reservoir and tributary habitats due to large drawdownregimes. Structures, reservoirs, and diversions impede wildlife movement (especially for large mammals)."

Under the priority action of developing a current habitat assessment map for priority fish and/or wildlife species in the Coquitlam watershed, it is included that "consideration should be given to potential impacts from available climate change predictions relevant to the specific habitats (i.e., wildlife movements).

Under the priority action to implement priority species- and habitat-related conservation 
actions in the following (or most recent) Recovery Strategies and Management Plans for species at risk that are known to be in the watershed, it is included that the action will "identify threats to migration routes during summer emergence and recommend or implement mitigation for these threats" for Western Toads.

Other.Area: Mount Edziza, Stikine River, Spatsizi Plateau Wilderness, Gladys Lake, Pitman River, Chukachida, and Tatlatui Protected Areas
Plan.Type: Management Plan for a Protected Area or Provincial Park
Plan.Name: Management Plan for Stikine Country Protected Areas
Connectivity.Information:
The plan states that "Stikine Country Protected Areas also provide some connectivity to other protected areas in 
northern British Columbia. Connectivity is important for maintaining the exchange of migrating and dispersing wildlife between those protected areas. The Chukachida Protected Area provides an undisturbed low elevation connection between Stikine Country Protected Areas and Finlay Russel Park and Protected Area, which lies adjacent to the east. The Pitman River Protected Area also provides an undisturbed low elevation connection between Stikine Country Protected Areas and high value wildlife habitat to the northeast."

Established management objective: coordinate with other agencies, First Nations, commercial operators and the public to minimize impacts on Stikin Country PAs' values caused by activities on adjacent lands within the Spatsizi Greater Ecosystem and Mount Edziza Greater Ecosystem; subsequent strategy: cooperate with other agencies, First Nations and resource industries to maintain regional connectivity for wide ranging large mammal species (e.g., caribou).

Established management objective: maintain naturally occurring, healthy and viable large mammal predator-prey systems in Stikine Country Protected Areas; subsequent strategy: "manage the caribou/Stones sheep/mountain goat/moose/wolf/grizzly bear/black bearpopulations in protected areas in the Mount Edziza Greater Ecosystem (Mount EdzizaProvincial Park, Mount Edziza Protected Area (proposed) and Stikine River Provincial Park  west of Highway 37) as the Mount Edziza predator-prey system, recognizing the connectivity and linkage between this area and the Spatsizi Greater Ecosystem, and the relatively closed nature of the caribou, Stones sheep and mountain goat populations."

Listed as a value to be protected are the key wildlife movement corridors along the Pitman and Chukachida Rivers which provide connectivity to high value habitat in the Mackenzie LRMP; subsequent management directions (see appendix 36).

Connectivity is not defined.
Connectivity.References:
Cassiar Iskut-Stikine Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP) - establishes management directions for protected areas within the Stikine Country Protected Area System, including connectivity.

Mackenzie Land and resource Managment Plan (LRMP) - Key wildlife movement corridors
along Pitman and Chukachida River provide connectivity to high value habitat in the Mackenzie LRMP.
Connectivity.Ref.Diag1: Cassiar Iskut-Stikine Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Connectivity.Ref.Diag2: Mackenzie Land and resource Managment Plan (LRMP)
Connectivity.Appendices: Appendix 36: Connectivity References in the Mangement Plan for Stikine Country Protected Areas
Corridor.Information:
The plan recognizes that the Pitman River and Chukachida Protected Areas contain wildlife migration corridors to high value habitat to the east; establishes management objectives to main the integrity of these corridors with several subsequent general directions that possibly pertain to corridors (e.g., ensure that any potential bridge across the rivers avoid crossing near important bull trout habitat) (see appendix 37).

Overall management direction: "The Stikine Country Protected Areas System is a core of protected areas for large mammal species in the region. Environmental Stewardship Division will work with other agencies and First Nations to coordinate management of known areas of use outside of protected area boundaries and to maintain wildlife movement corridors along the Pitman and Chukachida rivers."

Established management objective:  coordinate with other agencies and First Nations to minimize impacts on Stikine Country Protected Areas wildlife moving beyond protected area boundaries; subsequent strategy: "collaborate with the Ministry of Energy and Mines and the mining industry to minimize and mitigate impacts on the Pitman River and Chukachida River wildlife movement corridors."

Corridors are not defined.
Corridor.References:
Cassiar Iskut-Stikine Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP) - to partially protect the wildlife movement corridor along the Pitman Chukachida Rivers, the plan states it will follow this LRMP's management direction(s), also this LRMP lists the major movement corridors within Mess Creek and the Upper Stikine 
Spatsizi Extension as values to be protected.
Corridor.Ref.Diag1: Cassiar Iskut-Stikine Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Corridor.Appendices: Appendix 37: Corridor References in the Mangement Plan for Stikine Country Protected Areas
WildlifeMovement.Information:
The plan includes a section solely on wildlife movements; states that "although Stikine Country Protected Areas conserve over 1.4 million contiguous hectares of land and water, animals will continue to move beyond protected area boundaries and be influenced by management activities outside of the protected areas. Populations of large mammals such as grizzly bear, black bear, wolf, wolverine, caribou, moose, Stones sheep and mountain goat are likely to move beyond protected area boundaries for habitat or life cycle requirements."

The plan recognizes that there are velocity barriers at the lower end of the Firesteel River that prevent upstream movement of other fish.

Plan.Type: Land Use Plan
Plan.Name: Wóoshtin wudidaa Atlin Taku Land Use Plan
Publisher: Taku River Tlingit First Nation and BC Government
Connectivity.Information:
Maintaining landscape connectivity is listed as a factor in increasing resilience in the face of climate change.

Established management issue: "maintenance of connectivity to support movements and migration of species."

Established management objective for all RMZs: "maintain connectivity across the landscape"; subsequent implementation direction: "avoid, minimize, or mitigate impacts from resource development activities on natural patterns of species movement."

Conserving landscape connectivity with adjacent protected areas is a mangement itent for the Sloko River / Tlúk Aayi Héeni Resource Management Zone with several subsequent strategies (see appendix 44).

Maintaining ecological connectivity is listed as a management objective for all protected areas within plan region.

The plan defines connectivity as "Linkages between ecosystems and populations to form an interconnected network. At the landscape scale, connectivity refers to the degree to which a landscape facilitates or impedes movement of organisms among resource patches. The degree of interconnectedness and characteristics of connectivity linkages will vary in natural landscapes based on topography and natural disturbance regimes."

Connectivity is mentioned abundantly, but is not directly referenced/supported with citations.
OGWG_Connectivity.LegalTools.Methods.Imp:
Order of the Lieutenant Governor in Council 2014 (OIC 473) aka Atlin Taku Resource Management and Forest Retention Areas Order.

Park Act (Conservancies)

Land Use Plan
OGWG_Connectivity.Additional.Info: In 2009, land use objectives were established by an order that prohibited commercial forest harvesting in the areas of O'Donnel South, Hitchcock and Wilson Creek for a two-year period while a land use plan was being negotiated. Coinciding with the expiration of that order in 2011, management direction in the Plan was jointly developed and agreed to through government-to-government negotiations. In 2014, the Atlin Taku Resource Management and Forest Retention Areas Order was enacted to implement the Plan direction. The Order prohibits all hydro-electric development in the Racine Falls Resource Management Area (RMA) and limits hydro-electric development in three other RMAs (Hoboe-Willison Creeks / Sít'Héeni, Sloko River / Tl'uk Aayi Héeni, and Tulsequah Valley / Taas Teiyi Héenni). The Order also prohibits commercial forestry in large portions of the Plan area known as Forest Retention Areas (FRAs). However, commercial forestry is allowed to occur within the Atlin Taku Commercial Forest Harvest Area in the vicinity of the township of Atlin.  In the Wilson Creek Forest Retention Area, commercial forestry may occur for up to 30 years from the date of the Order, after which time the "no commercial harvesting" designation will apply. In addition to the 2014 Order, eight conservancies were established under the Park Act:  Upper Gladys River/Watsíx Deiyi, Golden Gate/Xáat Yádi Aani, Indian Lake  Hitchcock Creek/Át Ch'îni Shà, Monarch Mountain/A Xéegi Deiyi, Mount Minto/Kiyán, Taku River/Takú Téix, Tutshi Lake/Tooch Áayi, Willison Creek  Nelson Lake/Sít Héeni. As well, Atlin Mountain/Áa Tlein Shaa was added to Atlin Park, with the following areas under negotiation:  Kennicott Lake, Sheslay River, addition to Nakina-Inklin Rivers/Yáwu Yaa,  and Little Trapper Lake. Outside of legally established zones, the Plan is a policy document that provides direction to operational land and resource-based activities and guides planning processes at a more detailed scale.
Connectivity.Appendices: Appendix 44: Connectivity References in the Wóoshtin wudidaa Atlin Taku Land Use Plan
Corridor.Information:
The plan establishes that the identification of movement corridors between high value habitats prior to development activities that have potential to disturb goat/sheep from specified areas as a management direction under the objective of maintaining goat/sheep access to seasonally important habitats.

The Atlin River in the Atline Mountain protected area is recognized for providing a movement corridor between Taglish and Atlin Lakes for several fish species (e.g., lake trout).

The plan aims to  "maintain the remote wilderness quality and character and high recreational values of the 
Sheslay River corridor" as a area specific implementation direction for the Sheslay River Corridor Protected Area.

The plan includes a lists of recognized river corridors within the plan scope: Sheslay  Inklin  Taku River 
Corridor, 4th of July Creek, Consolation Creek, Gladys River, Whiting River, Tutshi River.

Corridors are not defined or supported with citations.

Plan.Type: Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Plan.Name: Bulkley Land and Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
"The LRMP is...  preferred [over the status quo harvest levels] when habitat indicators such as landscape connectivity, tree species composition, riparian habitat and conservation emphasis are measured."

Connectivity references as being important for young male mountain goats to access a wider area.

The plan lists landscape connectivity as a main component of managing for biodiversity that will be addressed within the plan.

Several management directions for the following units reference maintaining connectivity: Upper Nilkitkwa (planning unit 1), Babine River (plannning unit 2), Torkelsen (planning unit 3), Harold Price (planning unit 4), Babine Mountains (planning unit 5), Kitseguecla (planning unit 9), Telkwa (planning unit 11), and Copper (planning unit 12) (see appendix 45).

The plan states that ecosystem networks (ENs) within the plan area maintain connectivity and provide a representative cross-section of ecosystems.

The plan states that landscape corridors are designed to maintain connectivity within the landscape, but connectivity may not be required in all cases if a better opportunity to maintain older forest conditions and reduce fragmentation exists outside the corridor.

Connectivity is not defined.
OGWG_Connectivity.LegalTools.Methods.Imp:
Bulkley Objectives Set by Government 2006- Land Use Objectives Regulation (LUOR) (includes Landscape Corridors Objectives, Core Ecosystems Objectives)

Park Act 

Land Use Plan
OGWG_Connectivity.Additional.Info:
Management direction for the Bulkley LRMP outside of the Bulkley Valley SRMP area (aka Bulkley Valley Landscape Unit Plan). In this area, the Bulkley LRMP direction and the Bulkley Objectives Set by Government for the Bulkley TSA (2006) apply. 
In summary, the legal objectives can be found in:
1) Appendices 2, 3, and 4 of the Higher Level Plan Order, December 2000 and
2) Bulkley LRMP Objectives Set by Government, September 2006. Note that Objective 2.3 Mountain goat was replaced by UWR U-6-007 on August 15, 2019.  The objective in the original order under the LUOR was cancelled on June 11, 2020 (delayed due to timing of different process requirements for LUOR vs GAR orders). 
All other objectives and strategies from the plans are policy only.

The 2006 document, Bulkley Objectives Set by Government, was developed to streamline existing legal objectives from the Order Establishing RMZs and RMZ objectives within the Area Covered by the Bulkley LRMP (2000), Landscape Unit Plans, and the Bulkley Valley Sustainable Resource Management Plan, identifying any inconsistencies and developing new wording, consistent with the existing intent. 
The 2006 Order accompanying the Bulkley Objectives Set by Government replaces:
(i)   The 1998 biodiversity objectives for the Babine, Chapman, Copper, Corya, Deep Creek, Blunt, Harold Price, Nilkitkwa, Reiseter, Telkwa, Torkelson, and Trout Creek Landscape Units; and 
(ii)  Objectives in Appendix 1 of the Order Establishing RMZs and RMZ objectives within the Area Covered by the Bulkley LRMP (2000).

Although Appendix 1 of the Order Establishing RMZs and RMZ Objectives Within the Area Covered by the Bulkley LRMP (2000) is no longer in effect, the following remain IN EFFECT:
(i)   Agriculture / Wildlife Zone and objectives in Appendix 2;
(ii)  Special Management Zone 1 and objectives in Appendix 3; and
(iii) Special Management Zone 2and objectives in Appendix 4.
Connectivity.References:
Forest Practices Code (FPC) - establishes guidance for managing and maintaining biodiversity, which includes landscape connectivity as a component.

Babine River Interim Local Resource Use Plan (LRUP) - the plan states it will manage according to the guidelines set out in this LRUP, for which they state they will maintain connectivity with Sub-unit 1-2 in Babine River.
Connectivity.Ref.Diag2: Babine River Interim Local Resource Use Plan (LRUP)
Connectivity.Appendices: Appendix 45: Connectivity References in the Bulkley Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Corridor.Information:
The plan states that "landscape corridors maintain connectivity within the landscape, reduce habitat fragmentation, permit movement and dispersal of plant and animal species, and maintain forests dominated by mature tree cover."

The plan defines landscape corridors in great detail as a management tool and have been mapped for the Bulkley plan area (see appendix 46).

Established management direction for Babine River (planning unit 2): "In harvesting, restricting road access near areas where bears traditionally frequent is important as is maintaining habitat connectivity, especially along riparian and wetland corridors."

Within Telkwa (planning unit 11), management directions including the maintenance of wildlife and river corridors are established (see appendix 83)

Within Planning Unit 2: Babine River, management strategies have been established for Sub-unit 2-1: Babine River Corridor (see appendix 83).

The plan recognizes that grizzly bears use the Nilkitkwa River as a travel corridor to the Babine River.

Corridors are not defined.

WildlifeMovement.Information:
Within Planning Unit 7: Bulkley Valley - it is established as a specific direction for fish and wildlife habitat that agencies will focus management on winter habitat for ungulates,
migratory birds, and fisheries in the Bulkley and Telkwa Rivers.
Network.Information:
The plan includes ecosystem representation within the Ecosystem Network (EN) as a component of maintaining biodiversity that will have established management objectives and strategies within the plan.

The plan doesn't directly define ENs but states that "the ecosystem network extends throughout the Bulkley Plan Area in all planning units, and includes both core ecosystems and landscape corridors. Management strategies will range from no timber extraction to modified harvesting practices. These areas are not intended to have legislated boundaries; the boundaries are deliberately flexible to allow adjustment by the Forest District Manager and a designated Ministry of Environment, Lands and Parks (MoELP) official."

It is stated that "timber harvesting and silvicultural practices in the Special Management Zones and Ecosystem network will be modified to meet management objectives."

A comprehensive map for ENs has been included in the map, which is said to be a combination of core ecosystems and landscape corridors.

Ecosystem networks is referenced in the definition of landscape units: "design of ecosystem networks, visual resource management, and access management are examples of common activities of landscape-level planning."

Ecosystem networks are referenced in management strategies for several planning sub-units in the plan area (see appendix 84).

Forest Ecosystem Network (FENs) are referenced in management strategies for several planning sub-units in the plan area (see appendix 84).

FENs are not defined or supported with citations.

OGWG_Network.Additional.Info:
Management direction for the Bulkley LRMP outside of the Bulkley Valley SRMP area (aka Bulkley Valley Landscape Unit Plan). In this area, the Bulkley LRMP direction and the Bulkley Objectives Set by Government for the Bulkley TSA (2006) apply. 
In summary, the legal objectives can be found in:
1) Appendices 2, 3, and 4 of the Higher Level Plan Order, December 2000 and
2) Bulkley LRMP Objectives Set by Government, September 2006. Note that Objective 2.3 Mountain goat was replaced by UWR U-6-007 on August 15, 2019.  The objective in the original order under the LUOR was cancelled on June 11, 2020 (delayed due to timing of different process requirements for LUOR vs GAR orders). 
All other objectives and strategies from the plans are policy only.

The 2006 document, Bulkley Objectives Set by Government, was developed to streamline existing legal objectives from the Order Establishing RMZs and RMZ objectives within the Area Covered by the Bulkley LRMP (2000), Landscape Unit Plans, and the Bulkley Valley Sustainable Resource Management Plan, identifying any inconsistencies and developing new wording, consistent with the existing intent. 
The 2006 Order accompanying the Bulkley Objectives Set by Government replaces:
(i)   The 1998 biodiversity objectives for the Babine, Chapman, Copper, Corya, Deep Creek, Blunt, Harold Price, Nilkitkwa, Reiseter, Telkwa, Torkelson, and Trout Creek Landscape Units; and 
(ii)  Objectives in Appendix 1 of the Order Establishing RMZs and RMZ objectives within the Area Covered by the Bulkley LRMP (2000).

Although Appendix 1 of the Order Establishing RMZs and RMZ Objectives Within the Area Covered by the Bulkley LRMP (2000) is no longer in effect, the following remain IN EFFECT:
(i)   Agriculture / Wildlife Zone and objectives in Appendix 2;
(ii)  Special Management Zone 1 and objectives in Appendix 3; and
(iii) Special Management Zone 2and objectives in Appendix 4.

Plan.Type: Sustainable Resource Management Plan (SRMP)
Plan.Name: Bulkley Valley Sustainable Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
Ecosystem networks within the Bulkley LRMP provide wildlife habitat connectivity.

The plan states that landscape corridors provide habitat connectivity within the landscape - established management strategies of landscape corridors regarding connectivity: two subsequent strategies regarding connectivity management (see appendix 47).

Established management objective: In the Integrated Resource Management (IRM) Zone, retain structural diversity in managed stands through attributes of old forests, such as coarse woody debris and standing dead and live trees"; subsequent strategy: provide connectivity within the cutblock  through planing wildlife tree patches where possible.

Connectivity is defined within the plan as "a qualitative term describing the degree to which late-successional ecosystems are linked to one another to form an interconnected network. The degree of interconnectedness and 
the characteristics of the links vary in natural landscapes based on topography and natural disturbance regime. Breakage of these links results in fragmentation."

The plan includes connectivity under the definition of Forest Ecosystem Networks: "a zone that serves to maintain or restore the natural connectivity within an area."

Corridor.Information:
Established management objective: provide/maintain corridors of mature tree cover to proivde connectivity within the landscape and permit species movement; numerous subsequent strategies concerning landscape corridors and wildlife tree patches (see appendix 47).

Concerning stand structure management, the strategy of planning wildlife tree patches to provide connectivity within cutblocks when possible has been established. However, guidelines for these wildlife tree patches are not specified in detail.

The plan states that some harvesting is allowed in landscape corridors - a harvest decision matrix is included in plan that specifies regulations (see appendix 47).

Established management objective for deer cover: "maintain cover for security, visual screening, thermal cover, and snow interception needs within the identified key deer habitat"; subsequent strategy: "where feasible, achieve a visually screened corridor with forest cover retention along various ecotones (e.g., forest/field, forest/NCBr)."

The plan includes a map showcasing the landscape corridors that make up the Ecosystem Network within Bulkley Valley.

Landscape corridors "provide and maintain corridors (Map 4) of mature tree cover, containing structural and 
functional features associated with old forest, so as to provide habitat connectivity within the landscape, and permit movement and dispersal of plant and animal species by reducing habitat fragmentation.

Corridors are not defined directly, but landscape corridors are described thoroughly.

Network.Type: Ecosystem Network, Forest Ecosystem Network, Biodiversity Ecosystem Network.
Network.Information:
The plan states that "the ecosystem network (EN) is a key feature in the LRMP that provides for old growth retention, protection of the diversity of ecosystems (upland, riparian, and rare ecosystems), forest interior conditions, and wildlife habitat connectivity in the LRMP landbase. The network is comprised of core ecosystems and landscape corridors shown on Map 4. The EN was intended to be flexible, and to be modified based on new information and inventories. It may also accommodate new initiatives such as the designation of wildlife habitat areas, wildlife habitat management areas, ungulate winter ranges, and sensitive areas."

The plan includes a map showing the Biodiversity Ecosystem Network within the Bulkley Valley plan area (Map 4) - the Biodiversity Ecosystem Network is not defined.

The plan includes a procedure for amending Ecosystem Networks - the procedure must explain the reason for amendment (e.g., acquisition of more specific ecological information) and must suggest a suitable replacement (e.g., the proposed replacement area should be within +/- 5% ha of the area being replaced and should be connected to the existing ecosystem network).

The plan defines FENs as "a zone that serves to maintain or restore the natural connectivity within an area."

Plan.Type: Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Plan.Name: Cassiar Iskut-Stikine Land and Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan states that connectivity describes linkages between habitats, species, communities, and ecological processes.

Landscape connectivity is listed as an aspect of the general management direction (GMD) of this plan - "the objectives and strategies for GMDs provide a baseline for management of all resource activities on Crown land in the LRMP area."

The maintenance of connectivity of  is listed as a strategy for numerous management areas, such as fisher habitat, integrity of plateaus, caribou habitat, grizzly habitat, riparian areas, etc.

Includes a chart for the management directions for connectivity that features several areas where connectivity is specically being managed for (see appendix 48).

High connectivity values are recognized in the Hottah-Tucho Lakes and Middle Iskut zones.

It is stated that "Both the Pitman and the Chukachida provide important connectivity corridors for wildlife species comprising the predator-prey system up the rivers and their tributaries and into areas adjacent to the LRMP" - no logging or mining will take place in these areas.

Comprehensive amount of management objectives and strategies for maintaining connectivity.

Connectivity is defined within the plan as "a qualitative term describing the degree to which late-successional 
ecosystems are linked to one another to form an interconnected network. The degree of interconnectedness and the characteristics of the linkages vary in natural landscapes based on topography and natural disturbance regime. Breaking of these linkages results in fragmentation."

Legal implementation focuses on wildlife habitat

Wildlife Act s4(2)- Todagin Wildlife Management Area

Order - UNGULATE WINTER RANGE (mountain goat) #U-6-002 Nass TSA + Upper Portion of Ningunsaw and Unuk Watersheds (2008).
OGWG_Connectivity.Additional.Info: The Cassiar Iskut-Stikine Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP) was approved in the year 2000 based on the consensus recommendations of a multi-stakeholder planning table that included representatives of the Tahltan Nation. As a result of the LRMP, the Todagin Wildlife Management Area (WMA) was legally designated in 2001 by an Order in Council under the Wildlife Act. The principal management objective is to conserve Stones sheep populations and habitat, and other wildlife values integrated with mineral exploration and development. In addition, Ungulate Winter Ranges for Mountain Goats have been legally established by Order under the Government Actions Regulation on the border with the Nass South SRMP, south of the Cassiar Iskut-Stikine LRMP area.
Connectivity.References:
Biodiversity Guidebook - the plan cites the definition of connectivity from this document.

Riparian Management Area Guidebook - referenced to support riparian connectivity by ensuring consistent Best Management Practices (BMPs) are applied, reducing management uncertainty, and enhancing riparian reserve widths.

Mackenzie Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)  - the plan references key wildlife movement corridors along Pitman and Chukachida Rivers, providing connectivity to high value habitat in the Mackenzie LRMP; shares the same management directions.

Key movement corridors of several wildlife species are listed as values to be protected for the Tuya Mountains, Mess Creek, and Upper Stikin Spatsizi Extension protected areas.

River and wildlife movement corridors are both listed as biodiversity interests of the LRMP Planning Table.

Corridors are not defined.

Connectivity.Information:

Kaska Dena First Nations management consideration for mitigating the effects of climate change: maintain connectivity between conservation areas from north to south and low to high elevations.

Order Establishing the Dease-Liard Landscape Unit and Objectives
OGWG_Connectivity.Additional.Info: The Dease-Liard Sustainable Resource Management Plan provides resource management direction within the Dease-Liard Landscape Unit. It was developed in two phases. The first phase of the SRMP, with a focus on biodiversity, wildlife and timber, was approved in 2004 and a sub-set of objectives was legally established under the Forest Practices Code of BC Act. The second phase, approved in 2012, added chapters on minerals and aggregates; oil, gas and energy; tourism and recreation, access and climate change. The two plans together result in management direction for the full spectrum of resource values in the plan area, and the creation of the Ne'ah Conservancy. The Dease-Liard SRMP built on the Dease-Liard Area Assessment technical document that was developed between 1999 and 2001.
Corridor.Information:
For the Blue River and Dease River watersheds, caribou migration corridors are listed as a specific value of concern with the subsequent management strategy: "recommend that resource developers work to determine the extent and potential impact of development on areas having a high density of trails and traditional use sites as well as sensitive Stone?s sheep, mountain goat, caribou and moose habitat."

River corridors are referenced on several occasions, but mainly with a focus on recreational opportunites.

Large river corridors, as a tourism zone, as defined as "rivers that can be accessed by air, road, lake headed rivers, or access points within 1km of a paved road" - doesn't concern ecological corridors other than wildnerness qualities will be maintained.

Plan.Type: Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Plan.Name: Kalum Land and Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan lists connectivity as a secondary consideration as OGMA establishment in order or maintain old growth forest attributes.

Biodiversity objective: develop practical approaches to minimize habitat/population fragmentation; subsequent management strategy: establishment of connectivity corridors to ad in ecosystem network functionality.

Wildlife objective: maintain linkages and connectivity within/between watersheds; subsequent management strategy: provide suitable conditions to accommodate wildlife movement including seasonal travel through ecosystem networks.

Protected areas objective: increase co-operation between resource users adjacent to parks and park managers; subsequent management strategy: BC Parks will work with other agencies to promote connectivity between a park and wildlife habitat in surrounding areas.

Settlement zones objective: recognize conservation and other land use and resource management objectives when making decisions on the diposition of Crown land; subsequent management strategy: A co-ordinated approach to siting utility/transportation corridors will be promoted, particularly with respect to connectivity corridors, to minimize linear barriers to wildlife movement.

Connectivity is defined within the plan as "a qualitative term describing the degree to which late-successional 
ecosystems are linked to one another to form an interconnected network. The degree of interconnectedness and the characteristics of the linkages vary in natural landscapes based on topography and natural disturbance regime. Breaking of these linkages results in fragmentation."

Establishing grizzly bear management areas to provide landscape linkages is outlined within the plan (see appendix 85).

Connectivity corridors are not defined.
OGWG_Connectivity.LegalTools.Methods.Imp: Land Use Plan (see Kalum SRMP for legal implementation)
OGWG_Connectivity.Additional.Info: The Kalum Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP) recommendations were finalized and approved by Cabinet in March of 2001. Objectives and strategies from the LRMP were identified for establishment as Higher Level Plan objectives; however, the Higher Level Plan objectives document was neither finalized nor approved. For the most part, the LRMP is not legally binding and it remains as government policy. The Kalum Sustainable Resource Management Plan (SRMP) and land use order were approved in 2006. However, the Kowesas Landscape Unit is the only area of the Kalum LRMP that was not addressed by the SRMP. A separate planning process for the Kowesas Landscape Unit was started but not completed, therefore, the Kowesas Landscape Unit falls under the direction of the Kalum LRMP and a GAR Order for Mountain Goat Ungulate Winter Ranges.
Connectivity.References: Grizzly Bear Conservation Strategy (GBCS) - the plan references that GBMAs are the next level of stratification under this strategy; while connectivity is not mentioned with GMBAs, the maintenance of landscape linkages is described.
Connectivity.Appendices: Appendix 85: Grizzly Bear Management Area Linkages in the Kalum Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Corridor.Information:
The plan states that many of the agricultural areas within the Kalum planning area contains highly valuable wildlife travel corridors.

Established management strategies to maintain connectivity corridors (see connectivity column).

Community watersheds objective: maintain water quality through minimizing turbidity, sediment, and other contaminants; subsequent management strategy: Over time maintain/restore stream corridors to reduce erosion and sedimentation and to protect water channel stability. 

Corridors are not defined.
WildlifeMovement.Information:
There is an established management strategy to "evaluate current and plan future road location, construction  and deactivation activities such that development of linear barriers to wildlife movement is minimized."

There are established management strategies to: 1) "during resource development activities maintain the structure and function, for continued wildlife movement, through the level pass between the Kiteen and Cedar drainages identified on Map 7. Within the identified area 
on Map 7 retain 100% of the forested area located in polygon "A". Within polygon "B" of Map 7 timber harvesting will be limited to partial cutting systems," and 2) "in the low level pass between the Williams and Thomas/Clore watersheds facilitate wildlife movement by 
maintaining the structure and function of these wildlife opportunities."

Established management strategy to "reduce effects of disturbing accumulated sea-bed toxins by scheduling dredging for new or upgraded terminals so that it does not occur during anadromous fish movement or affect rearing and rearing habitat"

Established management strategy to "within a cut block, use riparian management areas, wildlife tree patches and reserves where they occur to provide furbearer movements across the cut block."

Established management strategy to " where possible, locate roads parallel to goat winter travel routes to avoid blocking winter movements."
Network.Type: Ecosystem Network, Forest Ecosystem Network, Biodiversity Ecosystem Network
Network.Information:
Biodiversity management objective: "develop practical approaches to minimize potential problems of fragmentation of habitats and populations"; subsequent strategy: "the Ministry of Environment, Lands and Parks will lead a technical working group to design strategies addressing habitat fragmentation, balancing ecosystem connectivity with timber supply. The group will recommend practical approaches, including  establishing connectivity corridors to support ecosystem networks."

Access management objective: minimize impacts of access on environmental, recreational, and cultural heritage values; subsequent management strategy: minimize construction of roads in forest ecosystem networks.

The plan includes the mention of biodiversity ecosystem networks in the management intent for ungulate winter ranges - "where possible, mountain goat and moose winter range will be incorporated into biodiversity ecosystem networks"; established objective: manage the Skeena, Nass and Beaver Moose Winter Ranges to sustain the over-wintering moose populations; subsequent strategy: "incorporate moose winter ranges in the application of ecosystem networks where such networks are designated."

Ecological networks are not defined within the plan.
OGWG_Network.LegalTools.Methods.Implemen: See Kalum SRMP- Connectivity and OGMAs support ecosystem networks

Plan.Type: Sustainable Resource Management Plan (SRMP)
Plan.Name: Kalum Sustainable Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The Kalum SRMP states that it addresses connectivity as an element of coarse filter biodiversity.

 objective 8 is to maintain forest stand structure and function for continued wildlife movement through the level pass between the Kiteen and Cedar drainages, and objective 9 is to maintain forest stand structure and function to facilitate wildlife movement in the level pass between the Williams and Thomas/Clore watersheds.

Connectivity of natural forest along/between streams and over low heights of land  is listed as a forest stand consideration under harvest and silviculture general wildlife measures for the SRMP.

"The Kalum LRMP recommends strategies to minimize potential problems of fragmentation of habitats and population. One such strategy is the establishment of connectivity corridors - two corridors are legally established at this time" - regarding this, objective 8 has been established to maintain forest stand structure and function for continued wildlife movement through the level pass between the Kiteen and Cedar drainages, and objective 9 is to maintain forest stand structure and function to facilitate wildlife movement in the level pass between the Williams and Thomas/Clore watersheds.

The plan includes a map of wildlife connectivity corridors for the plan area (map 5).

The plan defines connectivity as "a qualitative term describing the degree to which late-success ional ecosystems are linked to one another to form an interconnected network. The degree of interconnectedness and the characteristics of the linkages vary in natural landscapes based on topography and natural disturbance regime. Breaking of these linkages may result in fragmentation. Connectivity also refers to degree to which the condition of a landscape facilitates or impedes wildlife movement."

Connectivity corridors are not defined.

OGWG_Connectivity.Additional.Info:
The Kalum Sustainable Resource Management Plan (SRMP) implements some of the Kalum LRMP objectives and strategies by legally establishing them under the 2006 Order Establishing Land Use Objectives in the Kalum SRMP Area. This area encompasses the Kalum LRMP area other than the Kowesas Landscape Unit. The SRMP area contains legally established Wildlife Habitat Areas for Coastal Tailed Frogs, and Ungulate Winter Range for Mountain Goats and UWR for Moose (UWR 6-009) for the Kalum TSA, TFL 1 and TFL 41.
The Kalum LRMP directed that an ecosystem-based land management approach be piloted in key undeveloped watersheds in the Kalum SRMP area (Jesse, Emsley, Wathlsto, Hugh, Brim, Wahoo, and Owyacumish).
Part of the Kalum SRMP is within the southern boundary of the Nass Area and Nass Wildlife Area. The Kalum SRMP recognizes that the Nisgaa Final Agreement establishes a number of joint Nisgaa/Provincial/Federal committees to facilitate the planning of certain activities in those areas.

Plan.Type: Sustainable Resource Management Plan (SRMP)
Plan.Name: Nass South Sustainable Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
Per the plan's goals for water resources, effectiveness of small scale connectivity habitats through cutblocks is a retention level consideration of all streams riparian class S4.

The plan relates ecosystem networks, stand structure, wildlife tree retention area composition and size to connectivity management.

Listed plan goals for biodiversity: 1) maintain habitat connectivity throughout the landscape, 2) connect old-growth management areas, 3) provide a continuum of relatively undisturbed habitats that possess interior forest conditions for indigenous species that depend on mature and old-growth forests.

The plan lists the maintenance of structure connectivity as a biodiversity objective, and landscape connectivity is listed as a coarse filter biodiversity approach.

There is an established management objective to maintain structural connectivity in the ecosystem network"; several subsequent management considerations and targets (see appendix 50 and 86).

Connectivity through refugia and landscape-scale considerations is vital for wolverine, but the plan doesn't specify active management measures for connectivity.

Habitat connectivity is a factor that qualified professionals will consider upon discovering a goshawk or its nest.

The incorporation of moose winter ranges in the design/application of forest connectivity is listed as a best management practice for moose winter range.

For OGMAs, it is stated that connectivity values are a secondary consideration in the process of determining the location of new OGMAs.

Within the plan, connectivity is defined as "a qualitative term that describes the degree to which 
late successional ecosystems [old forests] are linked to one another to form an interconnected network... 
Breaking of these linkages results in forest fragmentation. Fragmentation due to forest harvesting should 
be viewed and managed to mimic fragmentation resulting from natural disturbance.

OGWG_Connectivity.Additional.Info: The Nass South Sustainable Resource Management Plan (SRMP), approved in 2012, is a landscape level plan developed to address sustainable management of land, water and resources in the southern portion of the Nass TSA. The SRMP was developed in partnership with the Nisgaa Lisims Government, Gitanyow First Nation, and the BC Government. The Nass South SRMP is consistent with the land use plan in the Gitanyow Huwilp Recognition and Reconciliation Agreement. A Land Use Objectives Regulation order was approved in February 2016, making many of the objectives legally binding for forest and range activities. The remaining objectives and management direction in the SRMP are not legally binding. In addition, an order establishing Old Growth Management Areas was made under the Environmental Protection and Management Regulation under the Oil and Gas Activities Act at the same time. As well, General Wildlife Measures for Mountain goats and Moose have been legally established under the Government Actions Regulation in approved Ungulate Winter Ranges (U-6-002 and U-6-018, respectively). General Wildlife Measures for Grizzly Bear Specified Areas were legally established in GAR Order #6-282.

Corridor.Information:
Under the plan's section on ecosystem networks, it states that ecosystem networks are intended to capture important wildlife movement corridors.

Plan goal for diversity: "to facilitate movement and dispersal of organisms across the landscape by providing core areas and dispersal corridors and will help a vareity of organisms re-colozine thier historic range."

Under the management objective to "maintain structural connectivity in the Ecosystem Network identified on Map 11: Nass South SRMP  Ecosystem Network, a listed management consideration is "in the Nass South Planning Unit, the 100-metre buffers plus the Ecosystem Network hydroriparian core reserve are intended to achieve, where possible, interior old forest conditions within sections of the EN but not necessarily the full length of the corridor."

Corridors are not defined.

The Nass South SRMP is consistent with the land use plan in the Gitanyow Huwilp Recognition and Reconciliation Agreement. A Land Use Objectives Regulation order was approved in February 2016, making many of the objectives legally binding for forest and range activities. The remaining objectives and management direction in the SRMP are not legally binding. In addition, an order establishing Old Growth Management Areas was made under the Environmental Protection and Management Regulation under the Oil and Gas Activities Act at the same time. As well, General Wildlife Measures for Mountain goats and Moose have been legally established under the Government Actions Regulation in approved Ungulate Winter Ranges (U-6-002 and U-6-018, respectively). General Wildlife Measures for Grizzly Bear Specified Areas were legally established in GAR Order #6-282.
WildlifeMovement.Information: In the plan's section on moose habitat attributes for life requisites, it is stated that "in areas of high snowfall, moose movement is facilitated by forests with crown closure of exceeding 50%."
Network.Information:
Within the plan scope, it states that the Nass South SRMP describes management objectives for "biodiversity, including... connectivity (e.g. ecosystem netowkrs)."

The plan outlines that it addresses coarse filter biodiversity partially through providing management directions for ecosystem networks.      

The plan defines Ecosystem Networks as areas that "capture biodiversity hotspots, high habitat values and important wildlife movement corridors, and serve to connect habitats across all elevations. Ecosystem networks also serve to shift the focus of forestry activities, from timber to be removed to timber to be left standing, such that a portion of the landscape has less emphasis on intensive forestry activities that alter natural stand structures, seral and patch size distributions."

The plan includes a table outlining the rationale for amending the ecosystem network (see appendix 86).

Under the plan's General Wildlife Tree Management Guidelines (appendix A), it is established that the plan will "retain high densities (30 percent or greater) of wildlife trees... throughout the harvestable portion of ecosystem networks."

There is an established management objective to maintain structural connectivity in the ecosystem network"; several subsequent management considerations and targets (see appendix 50).

Ecosystem networks are referenced as high value habitat for grizzly bears; "the designation of OGMAs, ecosystem networks and Protected Areas will contribute to old growth retention across landscapes, and thus the availability of grizzly bear forage."

Concerning management strategies for preserving mountain goat habitat/populations,  the plan states that "connectivity between alpine/lower elevation winter ranges and between landscapes for population dispersal is stated to be maintained through landscape planning."

OGWG_Connectivity.Additional.Info:
The Kispiox Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP) was approved in 1996 then amended in 2001 to incorporate land use decisions for the Upper Kispiox and Seven Sisters Planning Areas. The accompanying April 25th, 1996 Order Declaring the Kispiox Land and Resource Management Plan to be a Higher Level Plan (aka Kispiox LRMP Higher Level Plan Order) declared the provisions regarding resource management objectives and zones in sections 6 and 7 of the LRMP to be a Higher Level Plan under the Forest Practices Code of BC Act where they are applicable to operational plans. When the shift was made from the FPC to the Forest and Range Practices Act, it became confusing as to which objectives required results or strategies to be included in Forest Stewardship Plans. To clarify this, the District Manager wrote a letter on February 20, 2006 (file 12440-40/Kisp) re:  Clarification regarding Kispiox Land and Resource Management Plan and Higher Level Plan Order, dated April 25, 1996. In that letter, Barry Smith specified which objectives from the LRMP would require a result or strategy consistent with the Kispiox LRMP. To further clarify this, two new orders were approved in 2006 to represent "the functional interpretation of the Kispiox LRMP objectives for biodiversity, wildlife and visual quality" under the Forest and Range Practices Act. These orders are: 1) Order to Establish the Kispiox Landscape Units and Objectives (ONLY for wildlife and biodiversity) and 2) Order to Establish Scenic Areas in the Kispiox Timber supply Area (outside of Gitanyow and West Babine SRMP Planning Areas)
Both orders can be found in the document, Kispiox LRMP Higher Level Plan Objectives for Biodiversity, Visual Quality and Wildlife (January 2006). This document is sometimes referred to as the Kispiox SRMP. The 2006 Order to Establish the Kispiox Landscape Units and Objectives established all of the landscape units in the Kispiox LRMP plan area outside of the West Babine Landscape Unit (Cranberry, Kispiox North, Kispiox South, Lower Skeena, Middle Skeena North, Middle Skeena South, Upper Skeena, Gitseguecla, Suskwa and Babine). Objectives, indicators and thresholds/measures were established for biodiversity and wildlife for all of these Landscape Units except for the Cranberry Landscape Unit (planning for which later resulted in the Cranberry SRMP in 2012, for which legal objectives were established in 2016).
Connectivity.References:
Babine Local Resource Use Plan - includes strategies to maintain connectivity of grizzly bear populations in the region.

Forest Practices Code (FPC) - the plan uses the definition of biodiversity from the Code, then proceeds to state landscape connectivity as a key element of biodiversity that may be addressed when establishing landscape unit objectives.
Corridor.Information:
The plan contains the objective to protect the Babine River Wilderness Corridor, a "signficant unregulated river corridor with high salmonid values and critical grizzly habitat" - several subsequent strategies attached (see appendix 51).

Under the umbrella of several biodiversity objectives, the plan establishes a strategy to identify/map movement high value grizzly habitat which includes movement corridors.

The plan states that "connective corridors, for the purposes of wildlife travel and the maintenance of old growth and biodiversity, will be maintained at a landscape level in the Kispiox TSA. These corridors will be defined 
at the landscape unit and/or total resource planning level. This may or may not occur within the SMZ in the Upper Kispiox."

Important movement corridors for mountain goats are recognized to exist in the Coyote-Hells Bells GRD zone.

Moose movement corridors are recognized as an important aboriginal resource value in the Fiddler Creek area.

Corridors are not defined.

Connectivity.Information:
Within the plan's scope, it is said that the Cranberry SRMP describes resource management objectives for "biodiversity, including... connectivity (e.g. ecosystem networks)."

Listed plan goals for biodiversity: 1) maintain habitat connectivity throughout the landscape, 2) connect old-growth management areas, 3) provide a continuum of relatively undisturbed habitats that possess interior forest conditions for indigenous species that depend on mature and old-growth forests.

The plan lists the maintenance of structure connectivity as a biodiversity objective, and landscape connectivity is listed as a coarse filter biodiversity approach.

Established management objective: "maintain ecological functioning of streams, rivers, wetland complexes and lakes, including those that do not support populations of fish"; subsequent management consideration: "consider retention levels of 70% to 100% basal area on all streams of Riparian Class S4. Monitoring of retention levels to consider... effectiveness of small scale connectivity habitats through cutblocks."

There is an established management objective to maintain structural connectivity in the ecosystem network"; several subsequent management considerations and targets (see appendix 50).

Connectivity through refugia and landscape-scale considerations is vital for wolverine, but the plan doesn't specify active management measures for connectivity.

Habitat connectivity is a factor that qualified professionals will consider upon discovering a goshawk or its nest.

The incorporation of moose winter ranges in the design/application of forest connectivity is listed as a best management practice for moose winter range.

For OGMAs, it is stated that connectivity values are a secondary consideration in the process of determining the location of new OGMAs.

Within the plan, connectivity is defined as "a qualitative term describing the degree to which late-successional 
ecosystems are linked to one another to form an interconnected network. The degree of interconnectedness and the characteristics of the linkages vary in natural landscapes based on topography and natural disturbance regime. Breaking of these linkages may result in fragmentation."

Plan.Type: Sustainable Resource Management Plan (SRMP)
Plan.Name: Xsu gwin liklinswx: West Babine Sustainable Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
Connectivity is listed as a key management direction of this plan in landscape riparian corridors; subsequent management directions include restricting logging and no road building.

The plan lists landscape connectivity as a biodiversity strategy that will be implemted at both the landscape and stand scales.

The plan states that landscape riparian corridors concentrated on major tributaries to the Babine River have been mapped to provide connectivity of mature and old forest cover along valley bottom riparian areas and linking lowland to upland areas - subsequent management targets and considerations have been established (see appendix)

The plan states that wildlife tree retention targets have been identified to maintain structural features of old forests within harvested areas - "this increase is to ensure that larger openings maintain the structural and functional features required by species, including connectivity and habitat structure for small animals such as furbearers."

Connectivity for small animals is mentioned as a reason for greater wildlife tree retention requirements in cutblock patches exceeding 80 hectares.

The Kispiox LRMP contains the strategy of maintaining connectivity between alpine and lower elevation winter ranges for annual movements and between landscapes for population dispersal of mountain goats.

The plan defines connectivity as "A qualitative term describing the degree to which latesuccessional ecosystems are linked to one another to form an interconnected network. The degree of interconnectedness and the characteristics of the linkages vary in natural landscapes based on topography and natural disturbance regime. Breaking of these linkages may result in fragmentation."

The plan includes an appendix (appendix 6) that shows linkages between the Babine LRUP and the West Babine SRMP - connectivity and subsequent strategies and targets are listed (see appendix 87).

Connectivity is referenced under the definition of landscape corridors - "management zones that include riparian and linkage corridors which provide landscape connectivity."

Established management objective: "maintain the integrity of and linkage amongst critical grizzly bear habitats"; subsequent consideration: "critical habitats will be identified during operational planning," and target/measure: "no alteration of critical habitats, unless no practical alternative exists."

The Xsu gwin lik'l'inswx West Babine Sustainable Resource Management Plan (2004) was developed to implement the objectives of the Kispiox Land and Resource Management Plan (1996, amended 2001) and the Babine River Local Resource Use Plan within the Kispiox TSA. 
The Kispiox LRMP Higher Level Plan Order (1996, amended 2002) declares the provisions regarding resource management objectives and zones in sections 6 and 7 of the Kispiox LRMP, which overlaps the West Babine plan area, to be a higher level plan under the Forest Practices Code of BC Act where they are applicable to operational plans. The 2004 Order to Establish the West Babine Landscape Unit and Objectives and to vary the Atna/Shelagyote and Babine River Special Management Zone Boundaries establishes the West Babine Landscape Unit and objectives under the Forest Practices Code of BC Act. Resource Management Zones in the West Babine SRMP are made legal in the Amalgamated Map (Map 1) referenced in the  2004 Order and amended in 2012. The 2004 Order amends the objectives (but not the boundaries) for the Atna Shelagyote and Babine River Special Management Zones established  in the 1996 Kispiox Higher Level Plan Order.

Kispiox Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP) - the plan states that this LRMP states that connectivity between alpine and lower elevation winter ranges for annual movements and between landscapes for population dispersal, will be maintained through landscape planning.

The Landscape Unit Planning Guide - under listing landscape connectivity as a biodiversity strategy, the plan states that this guide was considered during the development of these objectives.

Corridor.Information:
Connectivity is listed as a key management direction of this plan in landscape riparian corridors; subsequent management directions include restricting logging and no road building.

Babine River Corridor Provincial Park runs along either side of the Babine River just downstream of Nilkitkwa Lake to Kisgegas Village; the Park was established to protect the wilderness values of the river corridor for fish, bears and wilderness recreation.

The plan states that landscape riparian corridors have been mapped to provide connectivity of mature and old 
forest cover along valley bottom riparian areas - meant to preserve landscape connectivity with several strategies in place (see appendix 52).

The plan states that some harvesting allowed in landscape corridors - a timber harvest matrix has been included (see appendix).

The plan includes that the mainstream of the Babine River functions mainly as a migration corridor for anadromous fish.

Babine River Corridor Park is managed through several  subsequent strategies (see appendix 52).

The plan states that the zoning of landscape riparian corridors will provide connectivity along riparian areas - included as a management direction for furbearer habitat.

Within a table summarzing additional factors and information that are to be considrered in developing a Forest Stewardship Plan within the plan area - regarding seral stage and the affect of development on biodiversity, a listed information requirement is " the amount of development within... landscape riparian corridors, including the proposed silviculture system."

The plan defines landscape corridors as "management zones that include riparian and linkage corridors which provide landscape connectivity. From the Treatment Units 1 and 3 of the Babine River LRUP."

Plan references a Babine LRUP objective to " Identify and maintain grizzly movement corridors to and along the river and areas outside the river corridor."

Landscape corridors are not exempt from mineral exploration or development, as mining activities are allowed outside protected areas under the Mineral Tenure Act.

Network.Type: Ecosystem Network (Core Ecosystems), Forest Ecosystem Networks
Network.Information:
The plan defines Ecosystem Networks as "core ecosystems and landscape riparian corridors" (see corridor column).

Core Ecosystems are defined as "management zones identified for the express purpose of maintaining structural and functional features of old forest ecosystems. Zone includes Old Growth Management Areas (OGMAs) and Treatment Units 2 from the Babine LRUP."

The plan states that "zoning for Core Ecosystems [will] not impede the right of First Nations to excercise their traditional activities."

Under the plan's section on biodiversity elements - seral stage distribution and old growth retention, "the Kispiox LRMP directs 12 per cent retention of old growth forest within each mid-sized watershed in the plan area.9 This percentage has been met by mapping Core Ecosystems (see Map 6, page 71). Treatment Units identified by the Babine LRUP (TU2, forest ecosystem networks) have been incorporated into these Core Ecosystems."

The plan states that no logging or road building will take place in established core ecosystems.

Established management objective: "to maintain the structural and functional features of old forest ecosystems within Core Ecosystems (see Map 6, page 71)"; subsequent indicators, targets, and considerations (see appendix 88).

Core ecosystems are referenced under the management considerations of three biodiversity objectives (see appendix 88).

The plan includes that many important habitat features for bull trout are buffered by Core Ecosystems.

Within a table summarzing additional factors and information that are to be considrered in developing a Forest Stewardship Plan within the plan area - regarding seral stage and the affect of development on biodiversity, a listed information requirement is " the amount of development within core ecosystems..., including the proposed silviculture system."

Core Ecosystems are not exempt from mineral exploration or development, as mining activities are allowed outside protected areas under the Mineral Tenure Act.

FENs are not defined.
