Plan.Type: Land Use Plan
Plan.Name: Klappan Plan
Publisher: Tahltan Central Government and the BC Government
Connectivity.Information:
No mention of connectivity.

The plan states that the Tahltan and British Columbia formed the Klappan Strategic Initiative (KSI) which produced a technical report of management considerations.

KSI Technical Report Management Considerations (appendix 1):
-Trapping: "resource development should provide an assessment of the implications of the development that considers habitat supply, forest succession/seral stage distribution and linkages/fragmentation, and temporal disturbance."

-Rare and Endangered Species and Ecosystems: "resource developments should provide an assessment of the implications on habitat supply, distribution and linkages/fragmentation of important wildlife habitats, and 
temporal disturbances that can conflict with critical seasonal habitats and migrations by alienating those habitats."

-Furbearers: "maintain a diversity of seral habitats with effective landscape level linkages in older coniferous stands for cover to support prey species and provide for critical habitats that will also maintain forest structure for furbearer denning and reproduction habitats."

OGWG_Connectivity.Additional.Info: The Klappan Plan was jointly developed and approved by the Province and the Tahltan Central Government on August 27, 2019. It covers 636,000 hectares in northwestern British Columbia and encompasses the area identified by the Tahltan people as the Sacred Headwaters, an immense subalpine basin which is the birthplace of three salmon-bearing rivers:  Skeena, Nass and Stikine. There are various forms of protection within the plan area including a Resource Review Area, No Registration Reserves, Oil and Gas Moratorium,  and a S.17 Land Act (Designated Use Area).

Plan.Type: Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Plan.Name: Lakes District Land and Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan states that Crown land allocations for settlement will avoid key environmental areas like biodiversity connectivity corridors. 

A coordinated approach will also be used to site utility and transportation corridors to minimize impacts on biodiversity connectivity and reduce ecological barriers.

The Lakes LRMP adopts the general management direction of maintaining ecosystem health based on an ecosystem approach to resource management; ensuring connectivity between important habitat types using naturally occurring corridors (e.g., riparian areas) is listed as an management strategy for maintaining biodiversity.

BC Parks will work with other agencies to ensure connectivity of wildlife habitat between parks and surrounding areas.

"Strong Links" are mentioned as a means to address connectivity concerns - function as interim FENs until landscape unit plans are in place.

Connectivity corridors are covered extensively and are included in numerous management strategies for managing general ecosystem health (see appendix 53).

The maintenance of landscape connectivity for quality caribou habiat in the Chelaslie Corridor and subsequent strategies are included in this plan.

Connectivity is referenced within the Chelaslie Caribou Migration Corridor Management Strategy within the plan.

The plan defines connectivity as "a qualitative term describing the degree to which late-successional ecosystems are linked to one another to form an interconnected network. The degree of interconnectedness and the characteristics of  the linkages vary in naturallandscapes based on topography and natural disturbance regime. Breaking of these linkages results in fragmentation."

*See Lakes North SRMP and Lakes South SRMP for implementation

Park Act
OGWG_Connectivity.Additional.Info: The Lakes District LRMP was completed for the entire Lakes Timber Supply Area in the year 2000. The Higher Level Plan Order for RMZs in Lakes District (2000) legally established some of the objectives from the LRMP. Objectives 1 - 5 and 7 were eventually replaced, but Objective 6 regarding management of the Lindquist and Chikamin Mineral/Wildlife Management Zone, is still in effect. Following the LRMP, two separate land use planning processes were completed  one for the southern portion of the TSA in 2003 (Lakes South Sustainable Resource Management Plan) and one for the northern portion of the TSA in 2009 (Lakes North Sustainable Resource Management Plan). Both SRMPs provide specific watershed-scale direction to legally implement objectives from the Lakes LRMP.
Connectivity.References: Biodiversity guidebook - the plan states that biodiversity and landscape connectivity objectives (Objectives 42 & 43) are not intended to create timber supply impacts greater than those suggested by the FPC Biodiversity Guidebook.
Connectivity.Appendices: Appendix 53: Connectivity and Connectivity Corridor References in the Lakes District Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Corridor.Information:
The Chelaslie area in the south of the Bulkley Basin Ecosection is a recognized critical caribou migration corridor - this plan includes numerous detail management objectives/strategies for management of the movement corridor (see appendix 54).

The plan states that "biodiversity will be managed consistent with LRMP direction and existing legislation 
and regulation including the Forest Practices Code (FPC)... with the exception of the Chelaslie Caribou Migration Corridor, where specific seral requirements have been identified for purposes of wildlife habitat management."

The southern portion of the Lakes District encompasses most of the summer and winter ranges, and all of the intervening migration corridor for the provincially significant Tweesmuir-Entiako caribou herd.

Crown land allocations for settlement will avoid key environmental areas like biodiversity connectivity corridors.

The Lakes LRMP adopts the general management direction of maintaining ecosystem health based on an ecosystem approach to resource management; ensuring connectivity between important habitat types using naturally occurring corridors (e.g., riparian areas) is listed as an management strategy for maintaining biodiversity.

Connectivity corridors are covered extensively and are included in numerous management strategies for managing general ecosystem healh (see appendix 53).

Management emphasis for Chief Louis and Uduk Lakes will be on caribou migratory habitat as per the Caribou Migration Corridor Special Resource Management Sub-Zone. Backcountry Lake objectives will be secondary to migration corridor objectives. 

The plan includes Socio-Economic/Environmental Assessment Summary Tables which show updated consensus management versus the base case scenario; it is listed in the 1997 updated consensus that "explicit direction to lower level plans to manage riparian corridors (Strong Links) as Forest Ecosystem Networks reduces risks and provides more certainty that habitat linkages and riparian landscape connectivity will be maintained."

The plan defines corridors as "a band of vegetation, usually older forest, that serves to connect distinct patches 
on the landscape. Corridors are part of the forest ecosystem network (FEN), and by providing connectivity they permit the movement of plant and animal species between what would otherwise be isolated patches."

WildlifeMovement.Information:
Under the objective to maintain caribou habitat (a component of maintaining a viable population), there is a subsequent strategy that "more detailed strategic plans will identify migration and wintering habitats and will incorporate information on caribou densities, habitat, and movement with a view to avoiding roads (if possible) or minimizing road densities within key areas."

Under the objective to maintain the diversity and viable populations of ungulate species and the habitats on which they depend, there is a subsequent strategy that "more detailed strategic planning will incorporate information on ungulate habitats and movement, with a view to reducing stress and displacement of wintering ungulates."
Network.Type: Forest Ecosystem Network, Biological Ecosystem Network
Network.Information:
The plan uses habitat linkages ("Strong Links") as interim Forest Ecosystem Networks (FENs) to maintain ecosystem connectivity and protect riparian habitats until landscape unit plans are finalized; these links reduce risks to species and ecological processes reliant on riparian areas in the short term and potentially the long term, compared to the baseline scenario. 

Management objective: maintain/enhance habitat connectiity at the landscape level; subsequent strateies: 1) "strong links" will function as interim FENs prior to landscape unit planning; and 2) Knowledge of landscape connectivity requirements will change through development of higher resolution field-based inventories - these more detailed inventories will then guide FEN design.

FENs are being actively considered and managed through strategic allocations and planning directions to maintain key habitat linkages, particularly in riparian and migration corridors.

The plan defines FENs as "a planned landscape zone that serves to maintain or restore the natural connectivity within a landscape unit. It consists of a variety of fully protected areas, sensitive areas and old-growth management areas."

Biological Ecosystem Networks are said to refer to the MELP 1:50,000 interpretive ecosystem inventory of the same name.

The plan designates Intensive Timber Management Areas (ITMAs) in low-constraint areas using Biological Ecosystem Networks and Visual Landscape inventories to enhance forest productivity, support intensive silviculture, and minimize conservation conflicts, with detailed design refined at the landscape unit level.

Plan.Type: Sustainable Resource Management Plan (SRMP)
Plan.Name: Lakes North Sustainable Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan includes that connectivity was addressed through collaboration between a Technical Working Group, First Nations, and consultants to define spatial locations and strategies for OGMAs and connectivity corridors

Connectivity is a listed biodiversity objective within the plan scope.

The plan includes connectivity as a way to conserve biodiversity - it does so "to allow for the movement of organisms across the landscape by providing key stepping stones.""

This plan claims to provide flexibility by allowing for the harvest of pine-leading stands in connectivity corridors while deferring non-pine-leading stands in the short term.

Connectivity objectives are stated to be legal objectives.

This plan outlines the use of a landscape matrix within the landscpae connectivity network for landscape planning - landscape connectivity network preserves corridors of connected habitats, facilitating species movement, providing refuge during disturbances, and supporting key ecosystem functions like terrestrial, aquatic, and groundwater connectivity (see appendix 56).

Structural and functional connectivity are partially defined: "structural connectivity considers the physical structure of habitat within a landscape, independent of any measure of species movement (i.e. coarse filter)" and "The focus of functional connectivity is based upon species requirements where they are known"

The plan states that "since habitat requirements for movement are known for very few species (e.g. ungulates and marten) the approach to managing connectivity described here should be considered a hybrid between functional and structural connectivity.  management focuses on retaining mature/old forest structures and prioritizing areas with overlapping high-value habitats."

Established management objective: maintain connectivity within the landscape connectivity matrix by deferring harvesting for 7 years in non-pine-dominant and hydro-riparian stands, avoiding rare plant communities, and allowing limited harvesting only for essential roadwork or non-MPB forest health risks; several subsequent strategies to achieve this objective (see appendix 56).

The plan includes two appendices for relevant to the landscape connectivity network (see appendix 55)

Network.Information:
Networks are only referenced in the context of the plan's landscape connectivity network (see connectivity column).

The landscape connectivity network  is described as a series of habitat corridors located in areas with linear assemblages or complexes of ecosystem-based structural components (EBS), which include vegetative cover, hydro-riparian ecosystems, and rare plant communities; the network aims to maintain functional landscape connectivity by providing continuous habitat for species that prefer mature or old forest habitats, offering refuge during disturbances, and supporting both terrestrial and aquatic connectivity.

The network consists of three key components: 1) Vegetative Cover important for biodiversity (VC), which includes diverse forest types, 2) Potential Hydro-Riparian ecosystems (HR), areas vital for hydrological and ecological functions, and 3) Potential Rare or Endangered Plant Communities (RE), which are areas likely to contain rare plant species; these components, along with other habitat types, form a matrix that supports functional landscape connectivity

The landscape connectivity matrix is derived from an earlier connectivity strategy known as the Biological Ecosystem Network from the Lakes LRMP.

Plan.Type: Sustainable Resource Management Plan (SRMP)
Plan.Name: Lakes South Sustainable Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
Connectivity is listed as a coarse filter biodiversity objective within the plan scope, and it provides clear objectives to ensure that a diversity of forest habitats are retained on the landscape by "providing connectivity, to allow for the movement of organisms across the landscape."

The plan states that beetle control management is allowed in Landscape Corridors that exceeds connectivity objectives. 

The plan describes connectivity networks - "connectivity networks contain natural vegetation, usually mature to older forests, that serve to connect distinct patches on the landscape thereby allowing easier movement of plant and animal species between, what would otherwise be isolated patches."

Management objective: Maintain within a managed forest setting, landscape corridors dominated by mature tree cover and containing most of the structure and function associated with old forest by  providing habitat connectivity andpermitting species movement; several subsequent strategies (see appendix 57).

Management objective: : Maintain structural diversity in managed stands by retaining Wildlife Tree Patches in each cutblock; subsequent strategy: distribute patches no more than 500 metres from one another to promote connectivity.

Several connectivity management appoaches discussed (Prince George, Bulkley, and Matrix Enhancemnt methods).

The plan includes an appendix titled Landscape Unit Planning Principles in the Lakes Forest District: Does the 
Mountain Pine Beetle Change Things? - the text emphasizes that maintaining connectivity objectives requires spatially explicit management due to the rapid harvesting caused by the mountain pine beetle infestation; it stresses prioritizing high-value habitats, such as wetlands, riparian areas, and winter ranges, for retention to support connectivity during this period and that retention strategies for connectivity should be reassessed in 3-5 years to ensure they remain effective amidst ongoing disturbances.

Connectivity is not directly defined - but it states that the goal "is to facilitate the flow of energy and organisms across the landscape."

Lakes South SRMP (2003) has ten objectives for biodiversity values in forested ecosystems. Objectives 1-8 were made legal in the Order to Establish Lakes South Landscape Units and Objectives (2003). In 2007, Objectives 2 and 3 from the 2003 Order were replaced by the Order to Amend Objectives 2 and 3 (Old Growth Forest Retention Objectives) Lakes South SRMP, which provides an updated objective for Old Growth Management Areas. Effectively, it spatialized the unmapped old growth. Further amendments were made to the OGMAs in 2016 to operationalize their boundaries. On November 21, 2019, Table 8 in the Lakes South SRMP was replaced with the Forest Planning and Practices Regulation section 66 targets. This is because Table 8 was only to be in place during the period of an accelerated AAC that addressed mountain pine beetle, which is no longer in effect.  The Lakes South SRMP also provides targets and strategies for management within Resource Management Zones established in the Higher Level Plan Order for RMZs in Lakes District (2000) and replaces RMZ objectives 1 - 5 and 7 in the HLP Order. However, objective 6 in the HLP Order regarding management of the Lindquist and Chikamin Mineral/Wildlife Management Zone, is still in effect.

Network.Information:
Forest Ecosystem Networks (FENs) are mentioned as a part of the Bulkley approach to connect core habitats across the landscape to provide connectivity; it is stated that elements of this approach is feature in the Lakes District LRMP.

In the plan's connectivity section, it is stated that "connectivity networks contain natural vegetation, usually mature to older forests, that serve to connect distinct patches on the landscape thereby allowing easier movement of plant and animal species between, what would otherwise be isolated patches."

In Appendix 6: Landscape Unit Planning Principles in the Lakes Forest District: Does the Mountain Pine Beetle Change Things? - it is stated that landscape level planning in the Lakes Forest District is required to implement the Lakes District LRMP and its associated Higher Level Plan, part of this direction can be summarized as "planning for connectivity to utilize the network of strong linkages as mapped in the Biological Ecosystem Network (BEN). Prior to landscape planning these strong linkages are to be managed to preserve connectivity (70% retention of mature/old forest cover and patch sizes below 2-4 ha). LRMP strategy to achieve connectivity directs landscape level planning to establish a network of landscape connectivity corridors based on this BEN template, providing opportunities for the distribution of species, populations and genetic material, as well as flow of nutrients and energy."

FENs are not defined or supported with citations.

No management objectives/strategies directly involving FENs.

Plan.Type: Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Plan.Name: Morice Land and Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan states that changes to the natural landscape patterns, including connectivity and distribution of seral stages and patch sizes from development, is a management issue.

Established management objective: maintain structural features important for the habitat needs of a vareity of wildlife species; "attempt to incorporate identified features into forested reserves. Where feasible and appropriate for the feature, maintain connectivity between features and mature overstory cover at block edges" by 2008.

The plan states that habitat connectivity along Friday, Nakinilerak and Hautête Lakes is maintained through a network of wetlands, creeks, and riparian ecosystems.

For the Grease Trail area, a loss of habitat connectivity is a listed issue and the goal of maintaining a functional connectivity corridor has been established; under the objective to maintain ecological structure and function adjacient to the trail - the implementation direction to "ensure connectivity is maintained within the forest matrix" is listed.

For the Nadina River area, associated terrestrial ecosystems are recognized to provide wildlife habitat connectivity.

The plan states that connectivity/connectedness across landscapes is poorly understood and thus will be considered/evaluated in the Morice Forest Ecosystem Management Strategy (MFEMS); management will focus on a heterogeneous range of seral stages and patch sizes to maintain connectivity over time.

Connectivity strategies/direction are given in the Interim Harvesting Guidelines for the Telkwa Caribou Herd Recovery Program Area for different zones (see appendix 90).

Connectivity and connectivity corridors are not defined.

OGWG_Connectivity.Additional.Info: A Morice LRMP recommendations package was developed between 2002 and 2004 as part of a multi-stakeholder process. Government-to-government discussions were held with the Office of the Wetsuweten, Lake Babine Nation, Nedoats Hereditary Chiefs and the Yekooche First Nation subsequent to the LRMP consensus in March 2004. The final LRMP was approved in 2007. On September 29, 2016, a Land Use Objectives Regulation Order established seven land use objectives for biodiversity (from LRMP, section 3.4.1) and an Environmental Protection and Management Regulation Order established Old Growth Management Areas (for the purposes of oil and gas activities). In addition, there is an Ungulate Winter Range for Mountain Goats (U-6-003, August 14, 2013) a UWR for Takla Caribou (U-6-013, January 4, 2021), a Wildlife Habitat Area (WHA 6-333, February 2016) for the Northern Caribou Telkwa herd, and Data Sensitive Species in place. The WHA 6-333 does not replace objectives 5-10 of the Morice LRMP.  Another WHA/UWR for the Whitesail Caribou herd is in progress. A Temperature Sensitive Stream designation process is also in-progress (as of September 7, 2017).

Interim Harvesting Guidelines for the Telkwa Caribou Herd Recovery Program Area - plan references harvesting guidelines pertaining to several characteristics, including connectivity, from this work.

Historic disturbance regimes of the Morice and Lakes Timber Supply Areas Working Paper - plan includes that this paper concluded that connectivity related to the distribution of seral stages would likely be related to stand initiating return intervals.

Corridor.Information:
Concerning mountain goat populations, the plan states that "opportunities for both dispersed and corridor goat movement between habitats"  is a management issue for the plan area; a subsequent established goal is the maintenance of "functional goat movement corridors that minimize the risk of mountain goat displacement and mortality." 

The plan recognizes that there is a large grizzly bear travel corridor running along the Nanika River; there is a listed management issue of "impacts to the grizzly bear travel corridor," and an established management goal to have "minimal impact to grizzly bears that utilize the river, both for feeding and as a travel corridor."

Under the objective to maintain ecological structure and function within the 500 metre buffer beyond the 100-year floodplain of the Nanika River - there is a listed implementation direction of: "mature and old forest retention is to be in contiguous units and distributed throughout the corridor."

Under the management objective to maintain the functional integrity of grizzly bear travel corridors, there is a listed measure/indicator of: "incidence of development activities impeding or displacing grizzly bear travel."

The plan recognzies that the Grease Trail and associated connected forested ecosystems form a unique travel corridor for wildlife species; plan discusses the connectivity corridor of the Grease Trail (see connectivity column).

For the Nadina River, under the management objective to "maintain ecological structure and function within the 500 meter buffer beyond the 100-year floodplain" - it is stated that upon completion of harvesting activities, new 
roads are to be decommissioned to only allow ATV access. 

Corridors are not defined or supported with citations.

Connectivity.Information:
Per the Bulkley LRMP, there is an included management objective for all FDUs to maintain habitat connectivity through landscape corridors dominated by mature tree cover associated with old forest; several subsequent strategies established (see appendix 95). 

Per the Bulkley LRMP - Higher Level Plan Order, there is an included management objective for the Jack Mould RMZ to maintain connectivity of mature forest between Kitseguecla and Jack Mould Lake; subsequent strategy: "the objective of maintaining connectivity of mature forest between Kitseguecla and Jack Mould lakes will be accomplished by following the Results and Strategies in 2.1.3.1.1 (Objective set by Government for Landscape Corridors)" (see appendix 95).

Per the Bulkley LRMP - Higher Level Plan Order, there is an included management objective for the Mooseskin Johnny Lake RMZ to "maintain ...forest connectivity between the Howson Range SMZ1 and Hankin Plateau SMZ1"; subsequent strategy: restrict road access within the RMZ.

Per the Bulkley LRMP - Higher Level Plan Order, there is an included management objective for Mulwain Creek RMZ to maintain forest connectivity within the Copper River SMZ2 by developing timber in a way that minimizes effects, restricting access, and designing cutblocks to natural landscape features; subsequent strategiy to follow the Results and Strategies in 2.1.2.1.1 (core ecosystem) and 2.1.3.1.1 (landscape corridors) (see appendix 95).

Connectivity is not defined.

Plan.Type: Forest Stewardship Plan (FSP)
Plan.Name: Coast Mountains Natural Resource District Forest Stewardship Plan
Connectivity.Information:
Per the Kalum SRMP objective to "maintain forest stand structure and function to facilitate wildlife movement, in the 
level pass between the Williams and Thomas/Clore watersheds identified on Map 5 [of the Kalum SRMP]"; subsequent results: To maintain the wildlife corridor in this area, the FSP holder's activities in the area 1) will be assessed by a qualified professional and recommendations will be followed, and 2) will be limited to maintaining access to timber that is otherwise isolated, removal of risk trees (e.g., insects, fire), and fire control.

Per management objectives from the the Nass South SRMP LUOR Order, strategies have been set that involve connectivity regarding wildlife, such as as marbled murrelet, goshawk, indigenous fish populations, and grizzly bear (see appendix 98).

Management objective: maintain structural connectivity in the Ecosystem Network as identified in Nass South SRMP - LUOR Order; several subsequent strategies (see appendix 98).

Connectivity and connectivity corridors are not defined.
Connectivity.References:
Kalum Sustainable Resource Management Plan (SRMP) - references a management objective from this SRMP where it establishes a result to maintain wildlife movement in a wildlife connectivity corridor in the plan area.

Nass South SRMP LUOR Order - plan references several objectives from this SRMP, for which connectivity is mentioned in the objectives and/or subsequent strategies.

Corridor.Information:
The plan manages the wildlife connectivity corridor in the Williams and Thomas/Clore SRM (see connectivity colum).

Under wildlife management objectives set by FPPR Section 7(1), there is a strategy established for moose habitat stating that timber harvest or road construction within the Wildlife Migration Corridor identified on FSP maps must be designed in consultation with a qualified professional to maintain corridor integrity.

Corridors are not defined.
Corridor.References:
Forest Planning and Practices Regulation (FPPR) - plan references wildlife objectives per the FPPR and subsequently references wildlife migration corridors in a strategy pertaining to moose habitat.

Kalum Sustainable Resource Management Plan (SRMP) - references a management objective from this SRMP, where it establishes a result to maintain wildlife movement in a wildlife connectivity corridor in the plan area.
WildlifeMovement.Information: Per the Kalum SRMP, there is an objective to maintain forest stand structure and function for continued wildlife movement through the level pass between the Kiteen (Ksi Gahltin) and Cedar drainages identified on Map 5 of the Kalum SRMP.
Network.Information:
Per general wildlife habitat managment objectives of the FPPR section 7(1), there is a subsequent strategy for Marbled Murrelet to follow strategies 3.7.8.1(Ecosystem Network-Hydroriparian Zone), 3.7.9.1 (Ecosystem 
Network-Structural Connectivity), and 3.7.10.1(Ecosystem Network-Buffer) during the period of this FSP (see appendix 99).

Per Nass South SRMP objectives regarding maintaining habitat for indigenous fish populations and the functional integrity of fish-bearing streams, rivers, and lakes, there is a subsequent strategy that "the FSP Holder will address the potential impacts of their harvesting and road construction activities to fish bearing streams through the adoption of Strategies 3.7.8.1(Ecosystem Network-Hydroriparian Zone), 3.7.9.1(Ecosystem Network-Structural Connectivity), 3.7.10.1 (Ecosystem Network-Buffer)" (see appendix 99).

Per the Nass South LUOR Order management objective to "retain 100% of the forested area of the hydroriparian zone within Ecosystem Network, as identified on Schedule I," there is a subsequent strategy that "the FSP Holder will ensure that during their operations 100% of the forested area of the hydroriparian zone within the Ecosystem Network identified on Nass South SRMP LUOR Order Schedule I is retained."

The plan includes the rationale for amending the Ecosystem Network within the Nass South LUOR Order in reference to retaining 100% of the forested area of the hydroriparian zone within the Ecosystem Network (see appendix 86).

Cited management objective and subsequent strategies to maintain structural connectivity in the Ecosystem Network identified in the Nass South SRMP LUOR Order (see connectivity column).

Forest Ecosystem Networks are not defined.

Plan.Type: Forest Stewardship Plan (FSP)
Plan.Name: Skeena Sawmills: Forest Stewardship Plan (Draft)
Connectivity.Information:
Referenced management objective: "maintain forest stand structure and function for wildlife movement through the level pass between the Kiteen and Cedar drainages, retaining 100% of forested area in polygon A and limiting timber harvesting to partial cutting in polygon B; subsequent strategy: 1) "within polygon "A", 100% of the forested area is retained," and 2) "within polygon "B", timber harvesting is limited to partial cutting systems."

Referenced management objective: "maintain forest stand structure and function to facilitate wildlife movement in the level pass between the Williams and Thomas/Clore watersheds identified on Kalum SRMP Map 5"; subsequent strategy: activities within the "Williams and Thomas/Clore SRM" wildlife connectivity corridor (Kalum SRMP Map 5) are limited to: 1) accessing otherwise isolated timber, 2) removing trees posing risks from insects, disease, or fire (with District Manager approval), or 3) fire control activities.

Referenced managment objective: maintain at least 30% of the perimeter around goshawk nests and post-fledging areas connected by mature or old forests to their foraging area. If the perimeter is less than 30%, use available forests and allow the most suitable adjacent forest to mature over time for connectivity, as determined by a qualified professional - several subsequent management strategies (see appendix 110).

Referenced management objective: maintain structural connectivity in the EN identified in the Nass South SRMP LUOR Order; subsequent strategy: "the FSP holder will ensure that, during the planning and development of cutblocks and roads under the authority of the FSP holder, structural connectivity within the identified EN - road construction within ENs is permitted only when necessary to access isolated timber, navigate terrain constraints, or when no alternative exists.

The plan defines connectivity as "a qualitative term describing the degree to which late-successional ecosystems are linked to one another to form an interconnected network. The degree of interconnectedness and the characteristics of the linkages vary in natural landscapes based on topography and natural disturbance regime. Breaking of these linkages may result in fragmentation. Connectivity also refers to degree to which the condition of a landscape facilitates or impedes wildlife movement."

Connectivity Corridors are not defined.
Connectivity.References:
Kalum Sustainable Resource Management Plan (SRMP) - plan references two objectives for maintaining connectivity in the Williams and Thomas/Clore watersheds and Kiteen and Cedar drainages.

Nass South SRMP LUOR Order - plan references objectives concerning connectivity management from this SRMP regarding goshawk and structural connectivity.

A Scientific Basis for Managing Northern Goshawk Breeding Areas in the Interior of British Columbia: Best		Management Practices - the plan references this work under the objective of maintaining connectivity for goshawk.
Connectivity.Ref.Diag3: A Scientific Basis for Managing Northern Goshawk Breeding Areas in the Interior of British Columbia: Best		Management Practices
Connectivity.Appendices: Appendix 110: Connectivity Management for Goshawk in the Skeena Sawmills: Forest Stewardship Plan (FSP)
Corridor.Information:
In the Williams and Thomas/Clore SRM wildlife connectivity corridor - there is an established result to limit activities within the SRM to: 1) accessing timber that would otherwise be isolated. 2) removing trees threatened by insects, disease, or fire, as agreed by the Coast Mountains Resource District Manager. 3) Fire control activities.

Corridors are not referenced outside of connectivity corridors, and are not defined.
Corridor.References: Kalum Sustainable Resource Management Plan (SRMP) - the plan references objectives concerning connectivity corridors from this SRMP.
Network.Information:
Referenced management objective: maintain structural connectivity in the EN identified in the Nass South SRMP LUOR Order; subsequent strategy: "the FSP holder will ensure that, during the planning and development of cutblocks and roads under the authority of the FSP holder, structural connectivity within the identified EN - road construction within ENs is permitted only when necessary to access isolated timber, navigate terrain constraints, or when no alternative exists.

Referenced management objective: "retain a 100-metre-wide buffer around the Ecosystem Network as identified 
on the Nass South LUOR Order Schedule I that meets the following forest conditions: 1) continuous forest cover, 
2) small discontinuous canopy gaps, 3) ?70% structure and function retained, including large, old trees, snags, and coarse woody debris, 4) multi-canopy levels, multi-aged forest, 5) 0% permanent road access, except where, for ecological or economic reasons, no other alternative is possible; subsequent strategy: the FSP holder will ensure that during the planning/development of cutblocks and roads that these conditions are followed within a 100-metre-wide buffer. 

Referenced management objective: "retain 100% of the forested area of the hydroriparian zone within the Ecosystem Network, as identified on Schedule I [of the Nass South LUOR Order]"; subsequent strategy: the FSP holder will ensure that during the planning/development of cutblocks and roads that 100% of the forested area of the hydro-riparian zone within the EN is retained, unless it is to be ameneded.

The FSP includes a table showing acceptable rationales for ecosystem network amendment (see appendix 86).

Plan.Type: Support Document for a Forest Stewardship Plan (FSP)
Plan.Name: Supporting Document for the Coast Mountains Natural Resource District Forest Stewardship Plan
Connectivity.Information:
Connectivity for Bull Trout is emphasized through the recognition of migration corridors as essential for their distribution and abundance.

Plan states that connectivity for fishers is critical; clearcut logging fragments habitat and reduces canopy cover. Maintaining riparian corridors and screening patches helps preserve habitat and reduce predator exposure. The BC Fisher Habitat Working Groups spatial data guides conservation efforts, ensuring management practices support connectivity needs for fishers and other furbearers.

"Grizzly bears are a wide-ranging species that depend on multiple, well-connected ecosystems."

Connectivity for the marbled murrelet is supported by maintaining old growth and reducing fragmentation through patch size distribution; strategies include preserving old growth proportions, designating Wildlife Habitat Areas, and establishing OGMAs - however no strategies are given.

Connectivity between Foch-Gilttoyees Park and Gitnadoix Park enhances habitat access within the murrelet's range.

Lack of specific management strategies/objectives referencing connectivity.

Connectivity is not defined or supported with citations.
Corridor.Information:
The plan states that corridors for Bull Trout are essential for their migration and habitat connectivity; the presence of migration corridors is one of five key habitat features influencing their distribution.

The Forest Practices Regulation supports riparian management areas, while wildlife tree patches and smaller openings enhance connective corridors for fisher.

The wildlife corridors identified for the Williams-Clore pass and the restrictions on the Kiteen-Cedar pass will provide protection for moose movement (as well as for other species); moose corridor management in the plan aims to sustain winter populations by enhancing habitat quality and facilitating movement; objectives include maintaining forage and thermal cover, ensuring security habitat, and managing corridors like the Kiteen-Cedar pass; road and cutblock developments will be evaluated by wildlife professionals, with mitigative actions, such as visual screens and forage management.

Concerning visual quality management - "the Kalum SRMP provides for a 100 m preservation corridor on each side of for the Upper Copper River."

The plan states that "the establishment of riparian management areas, sensitive areas, old growth management areas, ecosystem networks and buffers, and group and single tree retention will provide critical components of wildlife habitat such as... migration and dispersal corridors."

The plan references a pre-FPC plan known as the Thunderbird Integrated Resource Management Plan (TIRMP) that was integrated into the Kalum LRMP - it is noted that the "designation of a Special Resource Management (SRM) Zones (subzones 1 and 2) within the Kalum LRMP for the Lakelse River corridor. This designation was made legal with the establishment of the Kalum SRMP."

Corridors are not defined.
Corridor.References:
Kalum Sustainable Resource Management Plan (SRMP) - plan refers to this SRMP as it provides a 100 meter preservation corridor for the Upper Copper River, and for establishing legal designations for the Lakelse River Corridor.

Kalum Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP) - plan references this plan when talking about providing legal desinations of a special resource management zone for the Lakelse River Corridor.

Thunderbird Integrated Resource Management Plan (TIRMP) - plan references this plan when talking about providing legal desinations of a special resource management zone for the Lakelse River Corridor.
Corridor.Ref.Diag2: Kalum Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
WildlifeMovement.Information:
The plan states that "wildlife movement through low elevation passes is maintained, 
allowing species to forage over their normal range."
Network.Information:
The FSP highlights the establishment of ecosystem networks as crucial for wildlife habitat maintenance; they will enhance biodiversity by providing essential components like migration and dispersal corridors, wildlife trees, and structural diversity, supporting viable populations of native species.

Minimal mention and details of ecosystem networks within the plan.

Connectivity.Information:
Referenced management objective: "maintain connectivity of old and mature forest cover within Landscape Riparian Corridors as identified on Map 6, page 71 [of the West Babine SRMP]"; subsequent result:: the FSP holder adopts the Indicators and Threshold/Measure in Table16 as a result for Forest Connectivity in the West Babine FDU" (see appendix 10).

Per the FPPR s. 7(1), this FSP adopts the management objective "to conserve sufficient wildlife habitat in terms of amount of area, distribution of areas and attributes of those areas" without unduly reducing timber supplies; subsequent strategy: adopt biodiversity result 3.3.3.1  Result for Forest Connectivity in the West Babine FDU. (see appendix 10).

Management strategy for forest connectivity and access management in the Upper Kispiox Special Management Zone to maintain wildlife corridors and minimize ecological disruption; measures include: 1) limiting road density to 0.6 km/km² over 80% of the zone, 2) ensuring roads are temporary unless necessary, and 3) keeping sight lines under 300 meters. Forest harvesting is restricted to small openings, winter operations, and retention of fluvial units, with no activities allowed within 500 meters of alpine areas.

Established management objective and results for the maintenance of Northern Goshawk connectivity per the Cranberry SRMP (see appendix 110).

Established management objective: "maintain representative old forest structural connectivity throughoutthe forested landscape of Gitwangak Territory at a levelthat will result in a moderate or lowerrisk to the ecological functions and resilience of the represented ecosystems" - . the plan states that the until the Gitwangak 
Land Use Plan is approved by MinisterialOrder, this objective outlined in the Gitwangak Land Use Plan will be incorporated into Biodiversity Results and Strategies.

The plan defines connectivity as "a qualitative term describing the degree to which late-successional ecosystems are linked to one anotherto form an interconnected network. The degree of interconnectedness and the characteristics of the linkages vary in natural landscapes based on topography and natural disturbance regime. Breaking of these linkagesmay result in fragmentation. Connectivity also refersto degree to which the condition of a landscape facilitates or impedeswildlife movement."

Plan.Type: First Nation Developed Land Use Framework
Plan.Name: Kaska Dena Management Practices: Kaska Dena Land Use Framework
Connectivity.Information:
The plan lists connectivity is as an effective management practice that has been applied in other Northern Areas.

Connectivity is managed by the Kaska Dena through several general landscape strategies across different scales: 1) limit human activities and designate Protected Areas and Special Management Areas,  2) prioritize cultural and ecological values, ensuring that 70-80% of watersheds remain undisturbed, 3) key features like hunting areas and habitats are identified and linked within 70% of the area, ensuring connectivity through Large River Corridors and riparian management, 4) avoidance of key features, establishment of buffers, seasonal restrictions, and thresholds for disturbances are implemented to protect habitats, and 5) Kaska Dena will actively monitor resource development impacts to ensure sustainable development while preserving connectivity.

It is stated that riparian management might be increased to include providing connectivity to adjacent wetland complexes.

Connectivity is not defined or supported with citations.
Corridor.Information:
Under the management direction of conservation, it is stated that "land stewards will have the opportunity to identify features to maintain biodiversity and ecosystem function such as... migration corridors."

The plan includes guidelines for large river corridors - they are highighted as vital for biodiversity and cultural values; defined as rivers wider than 20 meters, these corridors require a 1 km buffer and aim to maintain over 80% in a contiguous forested state (see appendix 100).

It is stated that in large river corridors, human impacts will be assessed, and while limited timber harvesting may occur, strict guidelines will protect wildlife habitats and cultural sites.

Concerning Marten, establishes stand level practices for Marten involving corridors (see appendix 100).

Woodland caribou migration corridors recongized as being considered for management practices - management directions are included pertaining to caribou corridors (see appendix 100).

Non-riparian corridors are not defined or supported with citations.
Corridor.Appendices: Appendix 100: Corridor References in the Kaska Dena Land Use Framework and Kwadacha Nation Land Use Plan

Network.Type: Kwadacha First Nation Protected Areas Network
Network.Information:
A network of Kaska protected areas and special management areas based on cultural and environmental values in major watersheds have been identified.

Ecosystem and connectivity management direction: "Identify where wildlife habitats, cultural values, wetlands, lakes, streams, trails, old forests, hunting areas, and special elements listed in Table 1 are located in each watershed. Develop a connected network among the values"

Fish/wildlife management component: All parties agree to designate Kwadacha First Nation fish and wildlife management areas for protecting critical habitats and culturally important species, which will be incorporated into the Protected Areas Network.

Established management direction for woodland caribou: design a network of high-quality winter caribou habitat.

Plan.Type: First Nation Developed Land Use Plan
Plan.Name: Gitxsan Upper Nass Lax Yip Land Use Plan
Connectivity.Information:
This plan equates ecosystem networks to connectivity corridors - defined as corridors along rivers and streams that provide connectivity through the landscape and upslope to alpine areas; have been mapped in this plan using air photo interpretation.

The plan defines connectivity as "a qualitative term describing the degree to which late successional (older) 
ecosystems are linked (connected) to on another to form an interconnected network. The degree of interconnectedness and the characteristics of the linkages vary within natural landscapes based on topography and natural disturbance regimes. Breaking of these linkages results in forest fragmentation and may result in negative impacts to plants, wildlife, invertebrates that require connected older forest conditions to move through the landscape for foraging, dwelling, or migration purposes."

The plan defines landscape connectivity as "a qualitative term describing the degree to which late-successional ecosystems are linked to one another to form an interconnected network. The degree of interconnectedness and the characteristics of the linkages vary in natural landscapes based on topography and natural disturbance regime. Breaking of these linkages may result in fragmentation."

Plan recognizes that the majority of larger-river stream watersheds provide gentle to moderate gradients throughout the plan area, creating effective connectivity for wildlife migration.

Management consideration: advocates for 70% to 100% basal area retention along Riparian Class S4 streams to support habitat connectivity; monitoring will assess retention levels, windfall, stream conditions, and the effectiveness of connectivity habitats from cutblocks.

Riparian retention zones are meant to contribute to small-scale connectivity through the landscape.

"Forest landscapes that have historically been characterized by high levels of connectivity between old forests [in Upper Nass Lax Yip] will become increasingly fragmented by roads, young forests, and cleared lands."

Upper Nass interests for biodiversity management: 1) restoration and maintenance of landscape level connectivity throughout valley bottoms, valley bottoms to alpine, and beyond the area, and 2)  retain stand level structural diversity and connectivity within and surrounding harvested cut blocks and other industrial disturbances, and 3) Provide connectivity of all areas designated for management as Old Growth forests.

Established management objective:  Maintain representative structural connectivity throughout the forested matrix of 
Upper Nass Lax Yip at a level that will result in a moderate or lower risk to ecosystem function and resilience of the 
old growth forests"; several subsequent strategies (see appendix 101).

Plan states that it follows the rationale to maintain landscape connectivity to repsond to the effects of climate change on ecosystem functions.

Connectivity values are a management consideration of determining the location of old seral forests for retention for maintaining old, mature, and early forest seral stages that reflect the natural disturbance regime.

Management objective: "maintain structural connectivity in the Ecosystem Network identified on the Upper Nass LUP Ecological Resources Zonations Map; several subsequent management considerations (see appendix 102).

Abundant detail on how ecosystem networks provide landscape connectivity functions (see ecological network column).

Moose winter range will be protected with ecosystem networks that will enhance landscape connectivity for moose.

Established management intent for furbearers: "maintain high value habitat and landscape connectivity for identified furbearing species to help ensure healthy viable populations of the furbearers" - large areas of undisturbed land connected by corridors of undisturbed forest are required to provide refugia and landscape connectivity for fishers and wolverine."

Under the objective to ensure mining development is planned and carried out to protect ecological values, planning design and practices that may help achieve objective include avoiding disruption of large climate refugia and wildlife connectivity.

A management rationale for the objective of maintaining the effectiveness of water habitats and riparian habitats - "Additionally, wetlands and wetland complexes provide natural, effective routes for connectivity through the landscape. Disturbing the forest and soils of wetlands and wetland complexes can fragment the natural connectivity of the land into small refugia and inhibit the ability of some wildlife to move freely through the landscape and maintain biological diversity in a number of plants, invertebrates, and vertebrate species."

While connectivity is not cited in stand level retention management considerations - a listed rationale is that stand level retention "[provides]" connectivity between the developing stand and the original old forest, that can act as stepping stones for some organisms, allowing the organisms to achieve small scale movement within the stand 
and over time achieve landscape level movement."

Abundant mention and details on connectivity and connectivity corridors.
Connectivity.References:
Biodiversity Guidebook - the plan uses the definiton of connectivity from this guidebook.

Upper Nass Land Use Plan (LUP) - The Upper Nass LUP directs the maintenance of habitat connectivity in numerous ways, such as maintaining stand level structural retention and habitat patches; also establishes Ecosystem Networks (EN) that maintain landscape connectivity.
Connectivity.Appendices:
Appendix 101: Structural Connectivity Management in the Forested Matrix of the Gitxsan Upper Nass Lax Yip Land Use Plan 

Appendix 102: Structural Connectivity Management in the Ecosystem Network in the Gitxsan Upper Nass Lax Yip Land Use Plan
Corridor.Information:
Abundant mention of connectivity corridors (see connectivity column).

Listed management plan goal for biodiversity:  "facilitate movement and dispersal of organisms across the landscape by providing core habitat areas and dispersal corridors that will assist a variety of organisms to again inhabit their historic range."

The plan states that ecosystem networks protect/capture important wildlife movement corridors.

"Ecosystem networks (EN) of the Upper Nass LUP are landscape corridors focused primarily around streams, lakes, and wetlands" - it is listed that they capture important wildlife movement corridors.

The prescence of migration corridors are listed as a habitat feature that influences bull trout distribution and abundance.

Corridors are not defined.
WildlifeMovement.Information:
Under the objective to maintain a diversity of coniferous and deciduous species that represent the natural species composition of the landscape and stand levels, there is a management consideration to "use assisted migration; movement of populations within a species range to improve productivity, adaptation, and health in new climates; or move populations northward or upward in elevations beyond the species normal range to account for changes in site conditions (e.g. moisture becoming limiting, or Climate Change becomes warmer)."

A listed concern of Upper Nass Simgigyet regarding wildlife is "continuing fragmentation of forests and disruption to wildlife movement and migratory routes throughout the Upper Nass Lax Yip."

In the plan's section on moose habitat attributes for life requisites, it is stated that "in areas of high snowfall, moose movement is facilitated by forests with crown closure of exceeding 50%."
Network.Information:
The plan states that Ecosystem Network Core Reserves (corridors along rivers and streams that provide connectivity through the landscape and upslope to alpine areas) and Ecosystem Network Buffers (that provide a buffer to the EN core reserves) have been interpreted using air photographs and mapped with a 1:20,000 scale.

The plan states that the Upper Nass LUP directs the maintenance of habitat connectivity by maintaining Ecosystem Networks, among other elements.

Plan includes that "Ecosystem networks protect hydroriparian ecosystems, capture biodiversity hotspots, high habitat values, old growth management areas, important wildlife movement corridors, and Upper Nass cultural features, facilitate movement and migrations of plant and animal species and gene pools through the landscape, and serve to connect landscapes across all elevations. Ecosystem networks also contribute to achievement of mature-old seral stage andpatch size targets "

Includes a criteria that was used to identify Ecosystem Network core reserves in the plan area (see appendix 41).

Under the objective to "protect hydrologic integrity and manage risk to ecosystem function at a low level for all areas that are mapped as Water Management Units," there is a management consideration that 100% of the "Watershed areas identified on the Upper Nass Ecological Resource Zonation Maps  Hydroriparian Zone and Ecosystem Network Core Reserve, within which no industrial development is permitted to occur, excluding 
traditional cultural use, to be managed for water quality, quantity, and hydrologic integrity, biodiversity, habitat values, and cultural heritage values and us."

There is management notes that 1) "Where rivers, streams, wetlands, and lakes are included within Forest Ecosystems Networks or Water Management Units, the reserves and buffers will be as specified in Biodiversity Objectives and Water Management Unit Objective 3.0," and 2) "Where rivers, streams, wetlands, and lakes are not included within Forest Ecosystems Networks or Water Management Units, and are designed as an Upper Nass High Value Habitat, the reserves and buffers will include the full hydroriparian zone."

Under the objective to maintain representative structural connectivity throughout the forested matrix of Upper Nass Lax Yip, there are management considerations that: 1) "retention at the landscape level to contribute to the specified targets will include all forest land considered not appropriate for industrial development, including... Ecosystem Network core reserves and buffers," and 2) "Where additional retention is required to achieve the retention target, situate additional retention adjacent to the Ecosystem Network core reserve and Hydroriparian Zones to further aggregate habitat, protect water values, and increase connectivity throughout the landscape."

Under the objective to "maintain a distribution across the landscape of old, mature, and early forest seal stages that reflect the natural disturbance regime," there is a management consideration that primary considerations to determine the location of old seral forests for retention include areas already constrained from industrial development for other purposes such as Ecosystem Networks.

Management objective: "maintain structural connectivity in the Ecosystem Network identified on the Upper Nass LUP Ecological Resources Zonations Map; several subsequent management considerations (see appendix 102).

The plan includes a table showing the rationale for amending the ecosystem network (see appendix 86)

The intent of the Ecosystem Network is to provide connectivity in order to facilitate movement and dispersal of 
organisms through the landscape. The intent of interior forest conditions is to provide habitat conditions suitable for the persistence and movement of organisms that: 1) require the specialized habitats of old forest interior, 2) move very slowly through the landscape, 3) recover very slowly from disturbance (decades to centuries), 4) are rare species or in rare ecosystems.

In the plan's general wildlife tree management guidelines - it is a guideline to "retain high densities (30 percent or greater) of wildlife trees... throughout the harvestable portion of ecosystem networks."

Under the objective to maintain the effectiveness of: Water habitats, Riparian habitats, Brush patch habitats, and Upland forest habitats identified on the Upper Nass LUP Ecological Resource Zonation Map and the Wildlife 
Habitat Map as High Value Habitats for General Wildlife - there is a management consideration that "wherever possible, include the High Value Habitat polygons within a forest Ecosystem Network Core Reserve or an Old Growth Management Area."

The plan manages for 100% retention of the Ecosystem Network Core Reserve, Ecosystem Network Buffer (?70% retention), and High Value Habitat for General Wildlife, including critical moose winter range; the plan prohibits timber harvesting in these areas, except for minimal partial cutting in designated buffers.

Thre is a management consideration that the Ecosystem Network will contribute to maintenance of fisher, wolverine, and other furbearer habitat throughout the plan area.

The Ecosystem Network, including Core Reserves and Buffers, is mentioned to be vital for carbon storage, biodiversity, and climate change mitigation in the Upper Nass Lax Yip.

FENs are not defined.
Network.References:
Biodiversity Guidebook - the plan references that the guidebook defines connectivity, and subsequently Upper Nass LUP directs the maintenance of habitat connectivity by establishing Ecosystem Networks.

Forest and Range Practices Act - the plan states that cut blocks in place prior to the establishment of the ecosystem network is an acceptable rational for amendment under section 196(1) of the act.

Forest Planning and Practices Regulation - the plan states that cut blocks in place prior to the establishment of the ecosystem network is an acceptable rational for amendment under section 14(4) of this regulation.

Plan.Name: Management Plan for Kakwa Provincial Park and Protected Area
Connectivity.Information:
The plan states that "Kakwa Provincial Park and Protected Area and adjoining areas support a functioning predator/prey ecosystem, and ecosystem connectivity that allows free movement of species, and maintenance of genetic diversity."

No management strategies/objectives directly concerning connectivity.
Corridor.Information:
The plan states that the conservation role of Kawka PP and protected area is to provide travel corridors for blue listed (vulnerable) grizzly bears, Rocky Mountain bighorn sheep, mountain goat and northern (boreal) caribou. 

The plan includes that "McGregor and Providences passes and Kakwa Lake, appear to be of exceptionally high value as components of wildlife movement corridors. If further research substantiates this, special management considerations should be applied to the area to reduce disturbance."

Management objective: maintain/restore red and blue listed species; subsequent strategy: determine significance of McGregor and Providence passes and Kakwa Lake as wildlife movement corridor. If research substantiates this, special management considerations to reduce disturbance may be applied."

There is an included management strategy to limit recreational use along McGregor Valley in the park as it may impact wildlife movement corridors, also to not authorize any new cabins that would potentially cause conflicts with bears.

High priority strategy:  "further research is needed to determine... migration corridors, threats and recreational impacts to woodland caribou and mountain sheep." 

The plan states that the neighbouring West Twin Park occupies a section of the Rocky Mountain Trench between the two mountain ranges and provides a connecting wildlife movement corridor. 

Corridors are not defined.
Corridor.References: A Study of Grizzly Bear Movements, Corridor Design Attributes and Management to Minimize Grizzly-Human Encounters in A Protected Wildlife Corridor Across the Central Canadian Rockies, Kaka Provincial Park - recent work by Wayne McCrory on male grizzlies home ranges within Kawka PP.

and visitors, and the shared nature of resources such as wildlife habitat"; several subsequent strategies.

The plan states that research is being done to determine whether grizzlies migrate in late summer and fall to feed on salmon in the Fraser River system; this habitat and bear movement data will be used to help BC Parks minimize bear human conflicts.

Interprovincial wildlife movements is listed as a planning issue for the park.

Connectivity.Information:
The plan includes that the park will use an ecosystem-based management approach with an emphasis on managing for landscape connectivity in the park's vision statement.

Given the uncertainties inherent in climate change predictions and the response of biodiversity to the changes combined with serious biodiversity knowledge gaps, the plan states that a landscape connectivity approach is required.

Management objective: manage park values based on an ecosystem-based management approach; subsequent strategy:  Maintain close liaison with Parks Canada, provincial land and resource management agencies and private groups to conserve spatial connectivity..."
Corridor.Information:
Management objective: maintain/protect the natural divetrsity of wildlife species and populations; subsequent strategies 1) "work with Jasper National Park and other agencies to protect migration corridors for wide ranging species such as caribou and grizzly bear," and 2) "regulate domestic animals where necessary to protect park wildlife values (e.g., exclude dogs from overnight use of the Berg Lake corridor; and exclude domestic pack 
animal use other than horses to avoid possible transmission of disease to park wildlife)."

Management objective: "provide horse use opportunities that have minimal impact on the environment or conflict with other users; subsequent strategy: continue the closure on non-guided horse use in the Robson River drainage (Berg Lake corridor). 

The plan states that "it is essential that the movement of wide-ranging species is maintained to promote genetic diversity and the long-term viability of populations."

Corridors are not defined or supported with citations.

Management objective: maintain/protect the natural diversity of wildlife species and populations; subsequent strategy: "continue to monitor and evaluate ungulate mortality 
along Highway #16 and the railway and assess ungulate migration routes. Work with the relevant provincial government agency and CN Rail to reduce ungulate mortality."

Plan.Name: Fort St. James Land and Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan states that the Omnieca Protected Area is recognized to provide connectivity.

Biodiveristy management objective: "maintain the natural distribution as identified by biodiversity emphasis options within landscape units; subsequent strategy: "link important wildlife habitats and movement corridors to maintain connectivity across the landscape (Forest Ecosystem Networks, or patches) as appropriate."

Wildlife managementobjective: manage valuable habitats for a variety of species; subsequent strategy: implement strategies to maintain connectivity throughout the zone - however these strategies are not specified.

Connectivity is cited under the definition of a movement corridor: "... corridors are part of the Forest Ecosystem 
Network (FEN) and by providing connectivity permit the movement of plant and animal species between what would otherwise be isolated patches."

The plan states that "Birdflat Creek is one of the many drainages along the Sustut River that provide important wildlife movement linkages."

Connectivity is not defined or supported with citations.
OGWG_Connectivity.Additional.Info: All the LRMPs in the Omineca Region (Mackenzie NRD, PG NRD, Stuart Nechako NRD, Robson Valley TSA) were established as policy LRMPs in the late 1990s-early 2000s. After the enactment of FRPA (circa 2005), the objectives and strategies outlined within the Resource Management Zones of these LRMPs were mostly ignored and implemented as originally intended under these plans ceased. While the next level of landscape-level planning (e.g SRMPs, LUP), including habitat designations and old growth management designations was implemented through the establishment of  legal objectives, the outcomes for the management of these non-timber values were significantly negotiated to mitigate impact to timber supply. Additionally, even if FSPs referenced these LRMPs, there is significant uncertainty regarding their actual implementation, particularly connectivity, corridors, and networks objectives and strategies. Therefore, caution is advised when referencing for these LRMPs and their contribution to landscape integrity
Corridor.Information:
Mangement objectives to manage habitat/populations for mountain goats and grizzly bears with subsequent strategies directly involving movement corridors (see appendix 91).

Corridor Caribou Habitat areas serve as connective/migration corridors between caribou ranges - used as a management tool for movement corridors within plan (see appendix 91).

The establishment of connective corridors to reduce fragmentation is a management strategy to enhance furbearer/wildlife habitat.

"In the spring, caribou use lowland sites and migration corridors to feed on green vegetation."

The plan states that movement corridors are considered when locating new road access points.

"Corridors... will contribute to seral stage distribution and old-growth retention to meet biodiversity objectives."

The Stuart River is recognized as a major terrestrial/aquatic wildlife corridor, and Great Beaver Lake and the Salmon River are recognized as a wildlife movement corridor; also, the plan states that opportunities exist to provide movement corridors along Sowchea Creek.

The Tanizul. Mitchell, Inzana, Bait, Upper Omineca, Upper Sustut, Sustut, Damdochax RMZs recognized as providing movement corridors.

There istrategy to consider incorporating decidious stands along Dust Creek into a wildlife movement corridor in the Takla Lake RMZ.

Objective to maintain viability of movement corridors when coordinating access management for resource extraction in Squingula, Upper Sustut, Upper Omineca, Sustut, Damdochax, Groundhog RMZs.

Strategy to design a resource extraction zone along Sustut River and Bear River that recognizes/incorporates animal movement corridors in Lower Sustut RMZ; strategy for the Lower Sustut RMZ that calls to adopt movement corridors and linkages.

There is an listed management strategy for the Sustut RMZ that calls to adopt movement corridors and linkages.

The plan defines movement corridors as "a band of vegetation, usually older forest, which serves to connect distinct patches on the landscape. Corridors are part of the Forest Ecosystem Network (FEN) and by providing connectivity permit the movement of plant and animal species between what would otherwise be isolated patches."

Network.Information:
Established management objective for entire planning area: maintain natural distribution/abundance of plants/animals/ecosystems; subsequent strategy: link important habitats/corridors to maintain connectivitivty (e.g., FENs) as appropriate.

The plan establishes a strategy for the entire planning area to consider management of FENs when locating new roads for resource extraction.

Established management objective: maintain/enhance grizzly habitat/populations; subsequent strategy: use FENs to reduce habitat fragmentation.

The plan states that "through this LRMP, and other planning processes, the Ministry of Environment, Lands and Parks will provide a rationale for achieving landscape level biodiversity considerations (such as ecosystem networks)."

FENs are referenced under the definition of sensitive areas - "small areas designated to protect important values during forest and range operations. These areas, established by a Ministry of Forests district manager in 
consultation with a designated B.C. Environment official, guide operations on a sitespecific basis and require a combination of forest practices. Sensitive areas will be mapped by resource agencies, and include regionally significant recreational areas, scenic areas with high visual quality objectives, and forest ecosystem networks."

FENs are referenced under the definition of movement corridors - "corridors are part of the Forest Ecosystem 
Network (FEN) and by providing connectivity permit the movement of plant and animal species between what would otherwise be isolated patches."

FENs are not defined or supported with citations.

Plan.Type: Crown Land Plan
Plan.Name: Fort St. James Area Crown Land Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan recognzies that Wildlife Habitat management Areas (WHMA) provide landscape connectivity within the Crown land plan area.

A project is featured in the plan that estblished WHMAs per project outcomes (Fort St. James LRMP Arability Assessment/ Agricultural Development Area Project).

Connectivity is defined nor is it feature directly in management objectives/strategies.
OGWG_Connectivity.Additional.Info: The Crown Land Plans (CLP) of 2006 for the Mackenzie NRD, Stuart Nechako NRD (formerly the Ft. St. James and Vanderhoof Forest Districts), Prince George NRD, and Robson Valley TSA were developed with the primary goal of providing certainty for the agriculture industry.. This was achieved by legally establishing Agriculture Development Areas (ADAs) and Special Reserve Areas (SDAs) that used Arability Assessment information the LRMP references. The Wildlife Management Habitat Areas (WMHAs) referenced in the plans were legacy designations made under Section 16 of the Land Act in the late 1990s. These WMHAs, which were intended to protect fish and wildlife habitat and corridors, only prevent further land dispositions or tenuring activities under the Land Act. They do not prevent forestry activities under the Forest and Range Practices Act (FRPA), meaning logging has occurred in WMHAs. If these areas were logged, there is potential that the  objectives of the WMHAs designation has been compromised. A current assessment of the condition and effectiveness of these legacy WMHAs has not been completed. Caution should be exercised when referencing these WHMA designations, as it is unclear whether they still provide meaningful landscape connectivity or ecosystem integrity.
Connectivity.References: Fort St. James LRMP Arability Assessment/ Agricultural Development Area Project - establishes a WHMA that provides landscape connectivity.

Plan.Type: Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Plan.Name: Mackenzie Land and Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan states that the "conservation of biodiveristy depends on a coordinated strategy that includes... provision for a variety of habitats and habitat connectivity at the landscape scale."

Established management objective:  "manage for natural biological diversity by maintaining functioning and representative ecosystems across the Plan Area"; subsequent strategy: "link important habitats to establish or maintain connectivity in naturally occurring patterns across the landscape, recognizing that these links may vary over time, should not have a timber supply impact and must be accomplished through the strategic location of old growth management areas, partial cutting strategies and planning the distribution of cutblocks

Established management objective: "maintain habitat needs of all naturally occurring wildlife species across the Plan area"; subsequent strategy: "identify travel and connectivity corridors and, over time, manage them within the context of landscape level planning consistent with RMZ management direction."

Established management objective: "identify and manage to conserve grizzly bear habitat to assist in sustaining viable populations"; subsequent strategy: "designate critical grizzly bear habitats through existing legislative processes (Federal Endangered Species Legislation, Sensitive Areas, Wildlife Habitat Areas, Landscape Connectivity, Old Growth Management Areas, Protected Areas or localized features).

Established management objective for Management Zones: "maintain connectivity of important habitats using existing Forest Practices Code constrained areas (riparian areas, wildlife tree patches, old-growth management areas)"; subsequent strategies established (see appendix 92)

The plan defines connectivity as "a qualitative term describing the degree to which late-successional ecosystems are linked to one another to form an interconnected network. The degree of interconnectedness and the characteristics of the linkages vary in natural landscapes based on topography and natural disturbance regime. Breaking of these linkages may result in fragmentation."

Connectivity is referenced under the definition of Forest Ecosystem Networks: "a zone that serves to maintain or restore the natural connectivity within an area."

Connectivity is referenced under the definition of Vegetation Corridors: "corridors are part of the forest ecosystem network (FEN), and by providing connectivity they permit the movement of plant and animal species between areas that would otherwise be isolated."
OGWG_Connectivity.Additional.Info:
Legal designations for fisher and caribou in place for corridors (fisher) and migratory and calving for caribou.

Since the completion of the Mackenzie LRMP, there have been several UWR established for caribou for various objectives.  A 2024 cumulative impacts analysis on most of the Omineca UWRs is indicating that some UWR's have been impacted by anthropogenic disturbances (logging, mininig) up to 40-50% of the polgon area.  Contact Aija White for information on the UWR designations and CE impact assessment.
Connectivity.References:
Biodiversity Guidebook - the plan references that the conservation of biodiversity depneds on provision of habitat connectivity at the landscape scale, then references how the guidebook was developed to assist in implementing stand and landscape unit biodiversity across BC.

Landscape Unit Planning Guide - the plan references that the conservation of biodiversity depneds on provision of habitat connectivity at the landscape scale, then references how the planning guide was developed to assist in implementing stand and landscape unit biodiversity across BC.

Forest Practices Code (FPC) - the plan references maintaining ecologically constrained areas of the FPC in connectivity strategies.
Connectivity.Appendices: Appendix 92: Connectivity References in the Mackenzie Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Corridor.Information:
Established management objective: "support the management objectives and strategies for the Omineca area by conducting a detailed review of the Settlement Corridor following approval of the LRMP, with the intention of modifying the boundary; subsequent strategy: "identify wildlife movement corridors across the Omineca Settlement Corridor, between the north side of the valley and the riparian area, and where feasible, remove 
these lands from the Omineca Settlement Corridor and add to the protected area."

Mangement objective: "maintain habitat needs of all naturally occurring wildlife species across the Plan area"; subsequent strategy: indentify travel/connectivity corridors and manage them within the context of landscape level planning consistent with RMZ management directions.

Management objective: manage/perpetutate caribou and their habitats across plan area within the context of other wildlife species, continued resource extraction activities, more people, and existing and increased recreational use; subsequent strategy: consider establishing sensitive areas under the FPC for special caribou habitat sites such as high-use travel corridors).

The plan states that the Swannel, Baldy, and Manson Subzones are recognized as important corridors for caribou migration.

Established management objective for South Germansen/Upper Manson RMZ and associated subzones: "manage wildlife populations at sustainable levels to meet both consumptive and non -consumptive use levels, consistent with the management direction of each RMZ"; subsequent strategy: "Identify travel and connective corridors for caribou along the Manson and South Germansen rivers and, over time, manage to maintain these corridors within the context of landscape level planning. <Note: the intent is that the location of the corridors may change over time as long as some suitable corridor is maintained in each valley."

Animal migration corridors recognized as a Tsay ey Dene Traditional value for Lower Ospika SMZ in the plan; there are Tsay Key Dene management objectives established for numerous protected areas and special management zones to maintain important wildlife populations and habitats and migration corridors.

The plan defines Vegetation Corridors as "a band of vegetation, usually older forest that serves to connect distinct 
patches on the landscape. Corridors are part of the forest ecosystem network (FEN), and by providing connectivity they permit the movement of plant and animal species between areas that would otherwise be isolated."
Corridor.References:

The plan defines FENs as "a zone that serves to maintain or restore the natural connectivity within an area."

FENs are referenced under the definition of vegetation corridors - "corridors are part of the forest ecosystem network (FEN), and by providing connectivity they permit the movement of plant and animal species between areas that would otherwise be isolated."

FENs are not referenced in any management objectives/strategies.
