Plan.Type: Landscape Biodiversity Order
Plan.Name: Prince George TSA Landscape Biodiversity Order
Connectivity.Information:
This plan is for implementing the Order for old forest biodiversity management in the Prince George Timber Supply Area (PG TSA); it identifies and spatializes Candidate Old Forest Areas (COFAs) using ecological, cultural, and operational criteria to protect old-growth biodiversity, address cumulative effects, and incorporate First Nations' interests.

The plan states that COFAs in the plan area will provide functional old forest habitats and connectivity across the landscape and merged biogeoclimatic ecosystem classification units (mBECs).

Established management principle: fulfill the Ministry's commitment to the Forest Practices Board to spatialize the Order Establishing Landscape Biodiversity Objectives for the Prince George Timber Supply Area; subsequent considerations that involve connectivity: 1) "to maximize elevational connectivity between mBECs, proximity to already protected areas were considered while identifying the spatial location of candidate old forest areas," and 2) the spatialization prioritized large, intact patches of ecologically high-value old growth forest first, followed by near-old forests, while also considering younger forests where they enhance landscape connectivity or critical habitats.

Connectivity was considered for the biodiversity overlays of ecologically important and high value old growth forest, important areas to First Nations, and old growth forest patches (see appendix 93).

Connectivity has been considered in the methodology within the plan, but there is no futher management objectives/strategies referencing connectivity. 

Connectivity is referenced under the definitions of historic fires, ungulate winter range connectivity, and park connectivity - but connectivity itself is not defined.
OGWG_Connectivity.Additional.Info:
The spatialization of the PG TSA Biodiversity Order with COFA is only within a portion of the Prince George NRD that is outside of the CSFN ESI study area boundaries. The management of old growth within the CSFN ESI boundary remains non-spatial reporting. It is uncertain when the remainder of the PG TSA will be spatialized to meet the commitment to the FPB. 

The COFAs are non-legal under the District Management Letter of Expectation and remain this way until such time the Order can be amendmended or updated through a comprehensive planning process (i.e FLP, MLUP)
Connectivity.Appendices: Appendix 93: Connectivity References in the Prince George TSA Landscape Biodiversity Order

However, confusion surrounding the implementation, along with challenges in effective long-term monitoring, has led to significant harvesting in this corridor. Plan.Name: Robson Valley Land and Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan states that once the biodiversity emphasis is established for a landscape unit, the biodiversity guidebook provides recommended objectives for mature forest connectivity, among other things (e.g., patch size distribution).

Under several management objectives to maintain general biodiversity, established subsequent strategy: "forest harvesting patterns should be designed to maintain the connectivity of mature forests across and along all watersheds. Special emphasis should be given to maintaining mature forest connectivity across managed landscapes that connect protected areas."

West Twin PA contains a valley trench that is recognized to provided connectivity between ranges for numerous species (e.g., caribou).

The plan includes a chart comparing a summary of environmental accounts for the recommened land use plan versus the base case trends; concerning biodiversity, it is listed that the recommended land use plan provides improved connectivity by recommending FENs and maintaining mature forest linkages.

The plan includes future risk factors for the plan area: 1) landscape-level connectivity will decline over time due to cumulative impacts from forestry and tourism, and 2) resource development in the East and West Kinbasket, and Goat RMZs may reduce connectivity over time.

The plan defines connectivity as "a qualitative term describing the degree to which late-successional ecosystems are linked to one another to form an interconnected network. The degree of interconnectedness and the characteristics of the linkages vary in natural landscapes based on topography and natural disturbance regime. Breaking of these linkages results in fragmentation."

Connectivity is referenced under the definition of corridors: "corridors are part of the forest ecosystem network (FEN), and by providing connectivity they permit the movement of plant and animal species between what would otherwise be isolated patches."

Connectivity is referenced under the definition of forest ecosystem networks: "a planned landscape zone that serves to maintain or restore the natural connectivity within a landscape unit."

While the next level of landscape-level planning (e.g SRMPs, LUP), including habitat designations and old growth management designations was implemented through the establishment of  legal objectives, the outcomes for the management of these non-timber values were significantly negotiated to mitigate impact to timber supply. Additionally, even if FSPs referenced these LRMPs, there is significant uncertainty regarding their actual implementation, particularly connectivity, corridors, and networks objectives and strategies. Establish management objective for wildlife: maintain/enhance populations/habitat of wildlife; subsequent strategy: "identify and conserve travel corridors between alpine areas and low elevation habitat."

Several management strategy regarding Crown land and caribou corridors has been established for the Settlement/Agriculture RMZ in the plan area (see appendix 94).

The plan states that the Jackman Flats PA is a recognized travel corridor for deer, moose, and elk. and that West Twin PA is the only protected corridor across the Robson Valley trench which provides a caribou movement corridor across the valley floor and up East Twin/Fleet creeks.

Corridors are part of the forest ecosystem network (FEN), and by providing connectivity they permit the movement of plant and animal species between what would otherwise be isolated patches."

OGWG_Corridor.LegalTools.Methods.Impleme:
In the Robson Valley TSA, there are Old Growth Management Areas legal orders that include legal objectives for wildlife corridors for several streams around the Valemount area (Objective 3.0, Table 1). A seperate email of the legal order with reference to legal wildlife corridors will be provided. This was in response to the recommendations under the VAILUPD.

The Crown Land Plans (CLP) for the Mackenzie NRD, Stuart Nechako NRD (formerly the Ft. St. James and Vanderhoof Forest Districts), Prince George NRD, and Robson Valley TSA were developed with the primary goal of providing certainty for the agriculture industry. This was achieved by legally establishing Agriculture Development Areas (ADAs) and Special Reserve Areas (SDAs) that used Arability Assessment information the LRMP references.. The Wildlife Management Habitat Areas (WMHAs) referenced in the plans were legacy designations made under Section 16 of the Land Act in the late 1990s. These WMHAs, which were intended to protect fish and wildlife habitat and corridors, only prevent further land dispositions or tenuring activities under the Land Act. They do not prevent forestry activities under the Forest and Range Practices Act (FRPA), meaning logging has occurred in WMHAs. If these areas were logged, there is potential that the  objectives of the WMHAs designation has been compromised. A current assessment of the condition and effectiveness of these legacy WMHAs has not been completed. Caution should be exercised when referencing these WHMA designations, as it is unclear whether they still provide meaningful landscape connectivity or ecosystem integrity.

Plan.Type: Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Plan.Name: Vanderhoof Land and Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
In reference to natural disturbance types (NDTs) presented in the biodiversity guidebook, this LRMP establishes the management recommendation to 1)  Mimic the natural pattern of connectivity for natural disturbance types 2 and 3 that predominantly cover the plan area by establishing FENs to provide for movement corridors and special areas.and 2) provide landscape connectivity along riparian corridors.

The plan states that the application of FPC riparian management zones improves the connectivity over the 1995 Timber Supply Review scenario and the Entiako Protected Areas linking Tweedsmuir Park significantly improves landscape connectivity to the southwest.

It is stated that landscape linkages, such as wildlife corridors and Forest Ecosystem Networks (FENs), support biodiversity by providing habitats and facilitating species movement and genetic exchange; the Entiako Protected Area connects Tweedsmuir Park and indirectly links the Itchas Ilgachuz Protected Area, improving connectivity in the southwest.

Low-intensity resource management zones (e.g., Upper Blackwater, Laidman Lake, and Chedakuz RMZs) and riparian management zones in the plan area recognized to enhance landscape connectivity.

Plan states that connectivity remains limited in the eastern planning area due to a high emphasis on resource development and fragmented linkages, particularly where scenic or riparian corridors are absent or broken (e.g., Nechako River corridor at Vanderhoof).

It is acknowledged that connectivity remains limited in the eastern planning area due to a high proportion of high-intensity resource development zones.

The plan includes an environmental evaluation summary of the LRMP base case (previous plan) that states that: 1) "declining populations [of marten] expected in long-term with increased access, decreased habitat connectivity and decreased mature timber," and 2) "landscape connectivity improved over the [Timber Supply Review] in western portion by new proposed protected areas "

The environmental evaluation summary of the LRMP consensus plan (this plan) found that "increase in low 
intensity RMZ's and LRMP identified FEN?s improve landscape connectivity in the western portion over the Base Case."

Connectivity is not defined.
Connectivity.References: Biodiversity Guidebook - the plan references that the Guidebook provides direction for manaaging biodiversity by natural disturbance type (NDT); the LRMP provides management directions for maintaining connectiviy of these NDTs.
Corridor.Information:
The plan states that the corridor within the Stuart River RMZ provides high-value habitats (e.g., salmon, deer, elk).

The Finger-Tatuk RMZ contains riparian corridors that provide important habitat features (e.g., grizzly habitat).

Recommendation for natural disturbance types 2 and 3 that predominantly cover the plan area acknowledged by the LRMP: "Mimic the natural pattern of connectivity by establishing Forest Ecosystem Networks (FEN) to provide for movement corridors and special areas (e.g., wetland complexes, old growth attributes, etc.). The full spectrum of biogeoclimatic subzones and variants within a landscape unit should be represented. FENs should incorporate variable widths of linkages."

Regarding access management, the plan allows for loop roads and that "more detailed access guidelines need to be developed for wildlife, (i.e., protection of marten corridors, caves, etc.) similar in detail to those already in place for fisheries habitat and water quality protection."

The plan improves connectivity by identifying and protecting wildlife corridors, riparian reserves which provide travel corridors for many old growth dependent species, and low-intensity resource management subzones; these corridors suppor movements, particularly for species like moose, mule deer, and grizzly bear, and enhance the viability of adjacent Protected Areas.

It is stated that spruce-cottonwood habitats and wetland complexes serve as critical wildlife movement corridors, supporting species diversity and seasonal migrations; approximately 30% of wetlands and 23% of spruce-cottonwood habitats fall within Protected Areas or special resource zones.

The plan states that wildlife corridors serve two major functions in conservation: 1) they provide habitat for plants and animals, and 2) they act as travel corridors which provides for the seasonal movements and exchange of genetic material in wide ranging species.

The plan states that movement corridors create a more favourable outlook for moose - however no moose specific corridor strategies are established.

Corridors are not defined.

Network.Information:
The plan establishes a recommendation for natural disturbance types 2 and 3 that predominantly cover the plan area acknowledged by the LRMP to "mimic the natural pattern of connectivity by establishing FENs to provide for movement corridors and special areas - FENs should incorporate variable widths of linkages."

Within the plan's socioeconomic and environmental evaluation summary of the Vanderhoof LRMP consensus land use plan (this plan) versus the base case (previous plan), concerning biodiversity - "increase in low intensity RMZ's and LRMP identified FEN?s improve landscape connectivity in the western portion over the Base Case"; concerning wildlife habitats - "greater proportion of wetlands within low intensity development and FEN's in high intensity development areas."

Regarding timber harvesting, silviculture and forest health, the plan establishes a general management direction that: "Timber harvesting and silvicultural practices in Special RMZs or subzones, Sensitive Areas and Forest Ecosystem Networks will be highly modified to meet management objectives for other resources in these areas."

The plan states that the LRMP defined FEN's create a more favourable outlook for moose.

FENs are not defined or supported with citations.

Publisher: Community of Kwadacha and Dena Kayeh Institute
Connectivity.Information:
A goal of the plan is to maintain biodiversity over time - "the approach of maintaining regional, landscape, and stand level features and ensuring their connectivity across scales is considered a suitable method.

Established management direction regarding climate change: maintain connectivity between conservation areas from north to south and low to high elevations.

Established management objective: "maintain moose habitat at the watershed and stand level in within sub-basins of major watersheds"; subsequent direction: "assess areas to maintain the natural range of forested ecosystems over time and space, identify contiguous patches of moose habitat, and develop linkages between high value habitats to maintain large areas of moose habitats."

Established management objective: "maintain marten habitat at the watershed and stand level"; subsequent direction: "identify contiguous patches of marten habitat [and] place resource development outside of these areas or ensure forested linkages between high value habitats are maintained."

The plan states that increasing riparian management and reserve zones will provide connectivity to adjacent wetland complexs - but no management strategy specifics.

Connectivity is not defined or supported with citations.
Corridor.Information:

The plan states that when resource development is proposed in a Plan Unit, a coarse filter approach to maintaining biodiversity is required prior to development including defining at a plan unit scale concentrations of important habitat such as caribou migration corridors.

Corridor management objectives/strategies are referenced for moose, woodland caribou, and marten (see appendix).

The plan establishes that  timber harvesting should incorporate silvicultural systems and multi-block assessments tailored to site conditions and cultural values, emphasizing undergrowth protection and the enhancement of wildlife corridors.

Large river corridors have been defined as "a river generally greater than 20 m in width with the boundaries of the zone based on the hisorical flooding events, river morphology, alluvial ecosystems, enclosed or adjacent focal species habitats, and enclosed or adjacent Kaska cultural values"; management direction to maintain older forest in fire-resistant areas for biodiversity refugia in large river corridors.

Corridor information is not supported with citations.

Plan.Type: Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Plan.Name: North Coast Land and Resource Management Plan: Final Recommendations
Connectivity.Information:
Plan states that the connectivity of focal, blue, and red wildlife species is a listed representative target to be considered in land use management.

Management direction for aquatic/riparian ecosystems: planning development activities to protect values and functions by "buffering and maintaining connectivity of reserved areas to protect and enhance the form and function of reserves."

Management intent: maintain healthy and viable moose/deer populations; connectivity retention is a listed management consideration along with other approaches (e.g., wildlife tree patches, riparian buffers) for winter range management.

Management on-the-ground objective: maintain mature/old forest linkages within and between hydroriparian and upland areas at a watershed level - connectivity is listed as a consideration that will be factored in to all development activities (see appendix 75).

Management objective: maintain the integrity of and linkage amongst critical grizzly bear habitats; several subsequent strategies established (see appendix 75).

Connectivity is defined as "a qualitative term describing the degree to which late-successional ecosystems are 
linked to one another to form an interconnected network. The degree of interconnectedness and the characteristics of the linkages vary in natural landscapes based on topography and natural disturbance regime. Breaking of these linkages results in fragmentation."
Connectivity.References: Ecosystem-Based Management Planning Handbook (EBM Handbook) - emphasizes refining conservation targets by grouping ecosystems based on ecological criteria, including connectivity value. Connectivity is recognized as essential for setting thresholds that enhance ecological integrity while balancing human well-being.
Connectivity.Appendices: Appendix 75: Connectivity References in the North Coast Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Corridor.Information:
The plan recognizes that riparian corridors are unique and sensitive ecosystems that contribute to biodiversity within the plan area.

"Aquatic ecosystems of concern in the plan area include lakes, streams, wetlands and marine habitats. Management emphasis related to fish and wildlife (terrestrial and aquatic) reflects the importance of 
travel corridors, and feeding, breeding and rearing areas in and adjacent to water." - providing corridors for animal movement/plant dispersal is listed as a resource issue.

Established management objective for coarse filter biodiversity: "identify and reserve key wildlife migration/movement corridors"; subsequent considerations: 1) Identify and protect functional levels of habitat, travel migration corridors and breeding grounds, and 2) Ongoing inventory and monitoring of wildlife migration corridors - it is stated that management targets are not yet resolved for this objective.

Established management objective: sustain natural healthy ecological functioning of the complete range of hydroriparian ecosystems; subsequent management consideration: "hydroriparian areas are natural corridors for wildlife movements" - many development-related targets are established (see appendix 76).

The plan establishes a protocol for the examinination of movement corridors of Mountain Goat Winter Ranges.

Corridors are not defined.

Plan.Type: Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Plan.Name: Dawson Creek Land and Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan states that biodiversity conservation depends on protection and connectivity of large areas at the regional level, and provision of habitat connectivity at the landscape (watershed) level.

Butler Ridge, a goal 1 protected area, is recognized to provide valley bottom to alpine ecosystem connectivity.

Dawson Creek's river ripiaran areas/river valleys are recognized to provide habitat connectivity for ungulates, furbearers, bears, and migratory songbirds.

Established management strategy to identify and map connectivity corridors and/or FENs, and incorporate into landscape unit level planning is established for several RMZs for a variety of wildlife species, such as ungulates, trumpeter swan, and furbearers, and major river corridors (see appendix 63).

Important landscape connectivity between river corridors and uplands are recogized for various resource management subzones.

Few options remain to plan for landscape connectivity on Crown or privately owned land in agriculture/settlement RMZ subzones; where opportunities exist, a component of landscape connectivity and interior forest habitats should be sustained.

The plan defines connectivity as "a qualitative term used to describe the degree to which late successional ecosystems are linked to one another to form an interconnected network. The degree and characteristics of these linkages are determined by topography and Natural Disturbance Type (NDT). Specific types of connectivity are: upland to upland, upland to stream, upland to wetland, cross-elevational."

Connectivity is referenced under the definition of Forest Ecosystem Networks: "a planned landscape zone that serves to maintain or restore natural connectivity within a landscape unit."
Connectivity.References:
Biodiversity Guidebook - definition and specific types of connectivity are borrowed from this guidebook, and FENs are stated to serve or maintain natural connectivity in the guidebook.

Riparian Management Area Guidebook - definiton of riparian habitats is borred from this guidebook; they connect hillsides to streams and upper-elevation stream headwater areas to valley bottoms.

Corridor.Information:
The plan defines major river corridors as "lands within this Provincial Land Use category are identified major river valleys that have significant fish and wildlife habitat, recreation, tourism and scenic/visual quality values."

Abundant mentions of river corridors in terms of fish and wildlife, recreation, visual quality, culture and heritage, trapping, water quality, agriculture, mineral resources, energy, forestry, and access.

Numerous management objectives/strategies concerning major river corridors within the plan area (see appendix 64).

The identification and mapping of connectivity corridors listed as a strategy (see connectivity column).

The plan states that landscape connectivity between river corridors and uplands are important as these areas provide travel corridors for many mammals.
Corridor.References: Forest Practices Code (FPC) - plan states that the FPC sets out management requirements for protecting resource values within the river corridors, particularly in riparian areas.
Corridor.Appendices: Appendix 64: Major River Corridor References in the Dawson Creek Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Network.Information:
The plan establishes the identification and mapping of FENs as a management strategy under various wildlife objectives (e.g., manage ciritical caribou habitat) for the following fields: major river corridors, South Peace, Foothills, Plateau, Wildlife Habitat/Wilderness Recreation, Wildlife/Coalfield, Alberta Plateau, East Slopes,and Twin Sisters RMZs.

Networks are referenced in the management strategies for natural disturbance type 2: 1) "manage seral stage distribution in a variety of patch sizes within landscape units using a combination of clearcutting, partial cutting, aggregated group reserves and harvest units, and some mature and old seral stage forests in a connected network," and 2) "aggregate small-scale disturbances using clearcutting with group reserves in a contiguous network to simulate natural disturbances."

The plan defines Forest Ecosystem Networks as "a planned landscape zone that serves to maintain or restore natural connectivity within a landscape unit. FEN's are contiguous networks of representative old-growth and mature forest and are composed of a variety of protected and classified areas ( e.g., Protected Areas, old-growth management areas, riparian management areas and reserve zones, wildlife habitat areas and other sensitive areas such as unstable terrain, high visual quality or any other inoperable areas)."
Network.References: Biodiversity Guidebook - the plan uses the Guidebook's definition of FENs.

Plan.Type: Special Management Zone (SMZ) Plan
Plan.Name: Dunlevy Creek Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
Adjacent Butler Ridge Park recongized to provide valley bottom to alpine ecosystem connectivity.

Strategic forestry recommedation: "to facilitate wildlife migration and enhance wildlife habitat values associated with early seral forest, orient harvest openings / patches to achieve connectivity at a landscape level." 

Minimal mention of connectivity and lack of detail on how to manage it effectively.

Connectivity is not defined.
Connectivity.References: Forest Practices Code of B.C. Act's Operational Planning Regulation - plan references this document expressing that some forestry recommendations relate to practices that may be appropriate in a higher level plan for the SMZ (connectivity among recommedations, but is not specfically listed).
Connectivity.Ref.Diag1: Forest Practices Code of B.C. Act's Operational Planning Regulation
Corridor.Information:
Established strategic forestry recommedation: "to guide wildlife movement through early seral (juvenile) stands while reducing wildlife browse and associated damage to commercial regeneration on silviculture openings, reduce stand densities along identified wildlife corridors (e.g., create a gradation of stand densities through juvenile spacing to channel wildlife movement)."

Wildlife habitat recommendation: mineral licks are of special importance in the Plan area - "all activities should be conducted in a manner that maintains the functional integrity of licks and their associated animal approach corridors."

Corridors are not defined or supported with citations.
Corridor.References: Forest Practices Code of B.C. Act's Operational Planning Regulation - plan references this document expressing that some forestry recommendations relate to practices that may be appropriate in a higher level plan for the SMZ (wildlife corridors among recommedations, but is not specfically listed).
Corridor.Ref.Diag1: Forest Practices Code of B.C. Act's Operational Planning Regulation
WildlifeMovement.Information: The plan established that where alpine areas cannot be avoided, pipeline proposals must be designed and placed in a manner that is designed to accommodate wildlife (ungulate) movement.
Network.Information: Ecological networks are not referenced to or managed for.

Plan.Type: Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Plan.Name: Fort Nelson Land and Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan states that maintaining biodiversity depends on: 1) the conservation and connectivity of large areas as ecological benchmarks at the regional level, 2) providing habitat variety and connectivity at the landscape (watershed) level, and 3) management practices at the stand level.

General biodiversity objective: maintain biodiveristy throughout the plan area; subsequent strategy: "link important habitats to maintain connectivity across the landscape."

Wildlife management objective: maintain effective spatial/temporal habitat continuity; several subsequent strategies concerning connectivity (see appendix 65)

Management strategies involving connectivity established in the Dunedin, LaBiche, Smith River Uplands, Smith/Coal Rivers Corridor, Mile/Sulphur, Aeroplane Lake, Churchill, Moodie, Muskwa West, Rabbit, Sandpile, Stone Mountain, Terminal, Kechika River Corridor, Toad River Corridor, Turnagain/Dall Rivers Corridor (see appendix 65).

"By allocating so much of the western portion of the plan area (i.e., contiguous) as protected areas and Muskwa-Kechika Special Management Category with supportive management strategies, the Plan reduces the risks of regional fragmentation. This should provide for more natural levels of connectivity..."

Within the Toad River Corridor RMZ - managment objective: minimize habitat fragmentation; subsequent strategy: "Plan patch size, access and disturbance to emulate natural disturbance patterns. Utilizing aggregate cutblocks and clustered harvest patterns, focus on patch sizes at the upper limits identified in the biodiversity guidebook for this Natural disturbance Type. Stand level biodiversity to focus on riparian areas and wildlife tree patches."

The plan defines connectivity as "a qualitative term used to describe the degree to which late successional ecosystems are linked to one another to form an interconnected network. The degree and characteristics of these linkages are determined by topography and Natural Disturbance Type (NDT). Specific types of connectivity are: upland to upland, upland to stream, upland to wetland, cross-elevational."

Connectivity is referenced under the definition of Forest Ecosystem Networks: "a planned landscape zones that serve to maintain or restore natural connectivity within a landscape unit.
Connectivity.References: Biodiversity Guidebook - uses the guidebook's definition of connectivity, and references the guidebook for planning patch sizes to emulate natural disturbance patterns within the Toad River RMZ.
Connectivity.Appendices: Appendix 65: Connectivity and Connectivity Corridor References in the Fort Nelson Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Corridor.Information:
Plan establishes the Major River Corridors Sub-Category for Resource Management Zones (RMZs) to highlight the management of important values within river corridors; intent is "To manage for all the important values within the river corridors such as archaeological, cultural, energy resource, heritage, recreational, riparian, scenic, and timber resource."

Connectivity corridors have been  established as a management tool/strategy for several RMZs in the plan area (see connectivity column).

The plan states that "river corridor ecosystems contain some of the highest diversity of plants and animal species; provide critical habitats including home ranges, travel corridors, winter cover, nesting, foraging and reproductive sites."

Caribou Range RMZ - management objective: maintain forest attributes suitable for high capability low elevation caribou habitat; subsequent strategy: prevent the creation of predator-corridors (e.g., long tangents with extended lines of sight).

Contains numerous management objectives/strategies for Major River Corridor RMZs in the plan area (see appendix 66).

Corridors are not defined.

Corridors are referenced under the definition of riparian habitat - "riparian areas provide critical habitats, home ranges and travel corridors for wildlife and serve to maintain ecological linkages throughout the forest landscape by connecting hillsides to streams and upper-elevation stream headwater areas to valley bottoms."

Corridors are referenced under the definition of predator-prey systems - "any network of areas designed to maintain large predator-prey populations must include the centres of their occurrence and the vital linkages or movement corridors between them."
Corridor.References: Riparian Management Area Guidebook - references this guidebook in that riparian areas provide critical travel corridors for wildlife and serve to maintain ecological linkages throughout the forest landscape by connectivity hillsides to streams and upper-elevation stream headwater areas to valley bottoms.
Corridor.Appendices: Appendix 66: River Corridor References in the Fort Nelson Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
WildlifeMovement.Information: Muska River Corridor RMZ management strategy: identify and map migration routes along/within watercourses/waterbodies.
Network.Information:
Wildlife management objective: maintain effective spatial and temporal habitat continuity; subsequent strategy: Design connectivity corridors between important habitat areas where ecologically appropriate, ( e.g. using FENs).

The plan defines Forest Ecosystem Networks as a "planned landscape zones that serve to maintain or restore natural connectivity within a landscape unit. FENs are contiguous networks of representative old-growth and mature forest and are composed of a variety of protected and classified areas ( e.g., protected areas, old-growth 
management areas, riparian management areas and reserve zones, wildlife habitat areas and other sensitive areas such as unstable terrain, high visual quality or any other inoperable areas)."
Network.References: Biodiversity Guidebook - the plan uses the Guidebook's definition of FENs.

Plan.Type: Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Plan.Name: Fort St. John Land and Resource Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan states that maintaining biodiversity depends on: 1) the conservation and connectivity of large areas as ecological benchmarks at the regional level, 2) providing habitat variety and connectivity at the landscape (watershed) level, and 3) management practices at the stand level.

"Overall, the general management direction outlined in the Plan to incorporate ecosystem attributes into landscape-level plans, maintain connectivity, and minimize fragmentation indicate a reduced risk to biodiversity."

In the Besa-Halfway-Chowade, Bluegrave-Horshoe, Crying Girl, Graham South, Graham North, Grassy-Minaker, Kobes Creek Chinchaga, Trutch Creek, Redfern-Keily, RMZs connectivity, connectivity is mentioned in the management objectives/strategies of wildlife (grizzly bears, caribou) and biodiveristy; several subsequent strategies (see appendix 67)

Recognized important connectivity corridors within Grassy-Minaker RMZ are vital to such species as caribou, grizzly bears and migratory birds. 

Graham-Laurier PA recognized as containing high natural biodiversity (e.g. natural connectivity corridors between valley lowlands and alpine).

Proposed development in the Graham North and Graham South zones pose moderate risks to riparian connectivity.

Connectivity is not defined.

Connectivity is referenced under the defintion of Forest Ecosystem Network as a "planned landscape zones that serve to maintain or restore natural connectivity within a landscape unit."
Connectivity.References: Biodiversity Guidebook - the plan defines FENs from the Guidebook, which references how these networks maintain or restore natural connectivity.
Connectivity.Appendices: Appendix 67: Connectivity References in the Fort St. John Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
Corridor.Information:
Plan references major river corridors as "lands within this Provincial Land Use Category are identified major 
river valleys that have significant fish and wildlife habitat, recreation, tourism and scenic/visual quality values."

Contains management strategies involving incorporating connectivity corridors into landscale level plans for grizzly bear, caribou, and predator-prey systems (see connectivity column).

"Wildlife species may also use riparian corridors for migration and daily travel. Riparian habitats provide high capability winter habitat for elk, deer, moose, caribou, mountain sheep and mountain goat."

Contains management objectives/strategies for major river corridors within the plan area (see appendix 26).

The plan states that "lands within [the major river corridor special management category] are identified major 
river valleys that have significant fish and wildlife habitat, recreation, tourism and scenic/visual quality values."

The Graham-Laurier PA is reognized to have "high natural biodiversity (mix of forest cover types and age classes, moist riparian corridors, natural connectivity corridors between valley lowlands and alpine)."

Corridors are referenced under the definition of riparian habitat - "riparian areas provide critical habitats, home ranges and travel corridors for wildlife and serve to maintain ecological linkages throughout the forest landscape by connecting hillsides to streams and upper-elevation stream headwater areas to valley bottoms."

Corridors are not defined.
Corridor.References: Riparian Management Area Guidebook - references this guidebook in that riparian areas provide critical travel corridors for wildlife and serve to maintain ecological linkages throughout the forest landscape by connectivity hillsides to streams and upper-elevation stream headwater areas to valley bottoms.
Corridor.Appendices: Appendix 26: Major River Corridor Management in the Fort St. John Land and Resource Management Plan (LRMP)
WildlifeMovement.Information:
The plan states that "bull trout make extensive migrations through the main stems of the Halfway, Peace and Beatton Rivers. Populations of bull trout in the Halfway River watershed may be suffering from over exploitation and habitat degradation, linked to resource development activities."

Within Agriculture/Settlement Areas and numerous RMZ areas  - established fish management strategy to "identify and map critical fish habitat information ( e.g. pools, migration patterns, spawning and rearing areas).
Network.Information:
Established mnagement objective: maintain medium and high quality grizzly bear habitat; subsequent strategy: "minimize impacts on grizzly bear habitat by ensuring that critical habitat areas are linked by connectivity corridors or FENs" - Besa-Halfway-Chowade, Graham South, Graham North, and Kobes Creek RMZs.

Established management objective: maintain furbearer habitat for priority species (e.g., fisher, marten, lynx); subsequent strategy: where appropriate, incorporate landscape level forest ecosystem networks (FENs) to prevent priority species habitat fragmentation and maintain areas of interior forest habitat (e.g. > 600 metres wide) - Crying Girl, and Graham South RMZs.

The plan defines Forest Ecosystem Networks as a "planned landscape zones that serve to maintain or restore natural connectivity within a landscape unit. FENs are contiguous networks of representative old-growth and mature forest and are composed of a variety of protected and classified areas ( e.g., Protected Areas, old-growth management areas, riparian management areas and reserve zones, wildlife habitat areas and other sensitive areas such as unstable terrain, high visual quality or any other inoperable areas)."
Network.References: Biodiversity Guidebook - the plan uses the Guidebook's definition of FENs.

Plan.Type: Landscape Planning Pilot (LPP)
Plan.Name: Halfway / BC Landscape Planning Pilot
Publisher: Halfway River First Nation and BC Government
Connectivity.Information:
Connectivity is listed as a primary objective for resiliant landscapes; several management targets related to maintaining connectivity (see appendix 70).

Primary objective concerning the value of water to Halway River First Nation: "maintain function and connectivity of riparian habitat along rivers and streams"; several subsequent management targets (see appendix 70).

Management objective: maintain healthy wetlands; subsequent strategy: determine wetalnds and other waterbodies with no surface connectivity to other water features and develop a protection program. 

A further critical aspect of habitat management is maintaining the connectivity of wildlife trails and mineral licks - many strategies concerning wildlife are still under development.

Connectivity is a key focus of the Analysis program which is assessed through GIS tools, wildlife habitat mapping, and disturbance analysis (e.g., pipelines, roads) - goal is to ensure ecological corridors are maintained for wildlife movement and Treaty rights practices. 

Connectivity is monitored in the Fish and Wildlife Monitoring Program, which tracks disturbances to habitats, such as riparian areas, and evaluates their impact on connectivity and species survival.

Connectivity is not defined.
Connectivity.References: HRFN Forestry Guidelines - references these guidelines in the connectivity management table, but it is yet to be developed.
Connectivity.Appendices: Appendix 70: Connectivity References in the Halfway / BC Landscape Planning Pilot (LPP)
Corridor.Information:
The plan states that "restoration activities being undertaken by proponents within LPP Area #1 should be prioritized first within HRFNs Enhanced Management Corridors and within the Protection Zones established under the Gundy Plan."

Under the objective of people being able to move through lands for the practice of Treaty 8 rights, it is stated that known wildlife movement corridors ought to be restoration priorities - lack of specific strategies outlining this process.

Management objective: maintain function and connectivity of riparian habitat along rivers/streams; subsequent strategy: riparian area regulation updates, which would include ability for wildlife to move through said corridors.

Minimal management objectives/strategies directly concerning corridors.

Corridors are not defined.
Corridor.References:
HV1C Gundy Plan - plan references that restoration activities being undertaken by proponents within LPP Area #1 should be prioritized first within HRFNs Enhanced Management Corridors and within the Protection Zones established under 
the Gundy Plan.
WildlifeMovement.Information:
The plan establishes that fisher movement habitat should be maintained at 75% with total cover >20% as a management target.

The number of hectares of forest preventing ungulate movement (e.g., due to blowdown) is listed as a key metric of Water Management Basins (WMBs).
Network.Information: Ecological networks are not referenced to or managed for.

Plan.Type: High Value Plan (HV1)
Plan.Name: HV1-C Gundy Complex Plan
Publisher: Blueberry River First Nations and BC Government
Connectivity.Information:
Ensuring connectivity across the Gundy Complex and with adjacent areas is listed as a guiding principle of the plan.

Management goal: support recovery of moose habitat/populations within the Gundy Complex; subsequent priority measure: maintain connectivity of high value moose habitat for: winter forage/shelter, summer forage habitat, and mineral licks.

Habitat connectivity is identified as a Blueberry River First Nations (BRFN) restoration objective of the plan; "restoration activities should priortize work to restore...habitat connectivity in the Protection Zones over those in Development Zones."

Prior to oil and gas activities, proponents must consider and outline how connectivity regarding old forest, continguous diverse ecosystems, and moose habitat will be maintained - several strategies to demonstrate retention of connectivity included (see appendix 69).

Hydrological connectivity is a factor with determining location of borrow pits.

Maintaining moose habitat connectivity is a consideration for new linear infrastructure (e.g., pipelines); several subsequent management opportunities listed (see appendix 69).

The plan mentions planning for contiguous diverse ecosystems - defined as " a contiguous group of ecosystems (forest, non-forest, riparian, lakes) that are sufficiently buffered from anthropogenic edge such that they are sheltered from anthropogenic edge effects."

Computer modelling through the Marxan software was done in a way to identify the best areas for protection of connectivity values, such as connectivity corridors - provided information in the development of Protection and Development Zones.

Corridors are not defined or supported with citations.
Connectivity.Appendices: Appendix 69: Connectivity References in the HV1-C Gundy Complex Plan
Corridor.Information:
Plan states that it will aim to protect/recover several ecological values with the objective to focus on restoration of disturbed areas; priority restoration activities will take place within Enhanced Management Corridors identified by the Honey River First Nation. among other areas - the goal is to maximize the recovery potential for key ecological and cultural values, with a focus on restoring areas most impacted by previous activities and improving overall landscape integrity.

It is included that HRFNs Enhanced Management Corridors were a critical factor in establishing Protection Zones; where these corridors overlap with public land, they were largely incorporated into the protection areas to preserve cultural values. 

The plan states that "when considering offsets and other restoration opportunities, locations within HRFNs identified Enhanced Management Corridors should be prioritized."

In the Townsend HV1C area, it is stated that the main riparian corridor ought to be protected; pipelines crossing riparian corridors, especially on the west side and through the central valley, should be decommissioned to reduce fragmentation.

Corridors are not defined or supported with citations.
WildlifeMovement.Information:
Under pipeline guidelines, there is a management practice included to "adopt... wildlife movement-friendly designs, including, but not limited to avoiding hardscaping (e.g., concrete, asphalt, pavement) when permeable materials suffice (e.g., nature-based solutions), integrate small mammal and amphibian crossing structures into right of way post-construction remediation, integrate beaver deceivers, and ensure ditches and barrows will not entrap wildlife."

Management objective: support recovery of grizzly bear and other furbeaer habitat/populations; subsequent priority measure: Minimize impact to fur-bearer species habitat and movement, particularly denning habitat for fisher and marten.
Network.Information: Ecological networks are not referenced to or managed for.

Plan.Name: Forest Stewardship Plan For  BC Timber Sales Canadian Forest Products Ltd Louisiana-Pacific Canada Ltd West Fraser Mills Ltd Chetwynd Mechanical Pulp Little Prairie Community Forest Tumbler Ridge Community Forest In the South Peace River Region

Under objectives set by the government for wildlife and biodiversity - landscape level, regarding provincial non-spatial old growth objectives a subsequent strategy is that any recruitment strategy must consider an option that improves the protection of habitat (e.g. connectivity).

Minimal strategies regarding connectivity management.

The plan includes that "BC Parks will work with other agencies to ensure connectivity of wildlife habitat between [Gladstone] and [the] surrounding area and to identify areas of the park that may complement landscape unit planning biodiveristy objectives."

The plan references Gladstone and neighbouring park Granby as being important for regional connectivity.

Connectivity is not defined.
Connectivity.References: West Kootenay-Boundary Land-Use Plan (KBLUP) -  plan recommended policy direction on several issues involving the proposed new protected area; the government directed BC Parks to implement the following policy direction which has relevance for the  Park: "BC Parks will work with other agencies to ensure connectivity of wildlife habitat between the park and surrounding area..."
Corridor.Information:
Wildlife/ecological corridors not mentioned.

No management objectives or strategies referring to ecological corridors.

Plan.Type: Higher Plan Order to Another Land Use Plan
Plan.Name: Revelstoke Higher Level Plan Order
Connectivity.Information:
The plan prioritizes connectivity by incorporating it into seral adjustments, ensuring that forest stands remain ecologically linked across the landscape; when determining if a stand qualifies as mature or old seral, the spatial connection to surrounding areas is considered, preventing fragmentation.

For old growth conservation, the plan considers that connectivity is key in ensuring habitat integrity, especially for species like caribou, by avoiding isolated patches.

The plan states that critical connectivity areas have been mapped and tracked, ensuring they are preserved in forest management decisions.

Connectivity is not defined or supported with citations.
Corridor.Information:
Wildlife/ecological corridors not mentioned.

No management objectives or strategies referring to ecological corridors.

Plan.Name: Cranbrook West Recreation Management Strategy
Connectivity.Information:
Connectivity is not mentioned.

No management objectives or strategies referring to wildlife/habitat connectivity.
Corridor.Information:
Management objective: manage recreation access/development to ensure that the current distribution of fish and wildlife, the sustainability of their populations and the integrity of habitats, including water quality, are not compromised; subsequent strategy: work with landowners to maintain/establish wildlife migration corridors 
across private lands where fencing is diverting wildlife into dangerous/stressful areas.

Management objective: provide recreation opportunities that result in social, economic and 
cultural recreational benefits; subsequent strategy: ensure highway easements through private lands that are available for public access are open to public access and for wildlife migration corridors.

Corridors are not referenced or supported with citations.
WildlifeMovement.Information: General guidelines for all recreational users: 1) Learn about the types of wildlife that occur in your area and about their habitat requirements and migration patterns during different seasons, and 2) Avoid known sites used for breeding and rearing young. Watch for amphibians that are migrating between wintering and breeding areas, and avoid disturbing them.
Network.Information: Ecological networks are not referenced, nor are they managed for.

Plan.Type: Recreational Activities Plan
Plan.Name: Golden Backcountry Recreation Access Plan (GBRAP)
Connectivity.Information:
The plan poses aerial and ground-based recreational restrictions for specific management areas that have high connectivity values, such as for grizzly bears and caribou (see appendix 18).

Plan establishes aerial and ground-based recreational restrictions for specific management areas that are known wildlife or connectivity corridors, such as for grizzly bears or caribou (see appendix 18).

In the Ice River area, motorized use is restricted to key corridors; non-motorized areas (RA1) are designated to protect wildlife connectivity, while motorized use (RA3) is allowed in other areas, such as along the Black Road, to minimize disruption to grizzly bear habitat.

The plan recognizes that Bachelor Pass within the Bachelor Creek area is a historic cairbou connectivity corridor - it restricts specified recreation activities to manage for connectivity.

Recognizes that "an important grizzly bear connectivity corridor exists between the Ice River and Dainard Creek."

Connectivity and connectivity corridors are not defined.
Connectivity.References: Kootenay Boundary Land Use Plan (KBLUP) - indicates that the area should be managed to maintain/enhance opportunities for resorts and recreation, and maintain a range of recreational opportunities from motorized to non-motorized, while maintaining biological values such as ungulates and connectivity corridors.
Connectivity.Appendices: Appendix 18: Connectivity References in the Golden Backcountry Recreation Access Plan (GBRAP)
Corridor.Information:
The plan establishes aerial and ground-based recreational restrictions for specific management areas that are known wildlife or connectivity corridors, such as for grizzly bears or caribou (see appendix 19).

Plan recognizes there is a wildlife movement corridor from Kootenay River valley bottom to the Diana Lake area.

"Bush Pass [in the Valenciennes River area] is considered an important connectivity corridor between Banff National Park and the Golden TSA (one of the few low elevation passes between these jurisdictions)."

In the Bush River area, Thompson Pass will be classified as a very High Sensitivity to aerial landings, with no landings preferred (RA1 mapping designation) - due to wildlife corridor values adjacent to Banff National Park.

"The lower motorized (RA3) area (between the Moonraker area and the Columbia River) may be a key future town development area. One factor to be considered, however is that is has been an important spring elk migration corridor adjacent to the Columbia WMA."

"A lower elevation corridor extending from the Moonraker area to Donald, will be zoned as RM 1 to 
manage for ungulate and other conservation values upslope of the Columbia WMA" - RM 1 means areas with low development.

"A ridge between Canyon Creek and the Tallis Creek area, extending down to the Columbia River, will be zoned as RM 1 to manage for a wildlife movement corridor."

Plan includes that the northern area of Mount 7, affected by recent clearcuts, has raised concerns about the loss of mountain goat movement corridors due to timber harvesting and motorized use - motorized access is permitted here because the area is close to town and part of established public and commercial motorized use patterns.

Corridors are not defined.
Corridor.References:
Kootenay Boundary Land Use Plan (KBLUP) - indicates that the area should be managed to maintain/enhance opportunities for resorts and recreation, and maintain a range of recreational opportunities from motorized to non-motorized, while maintaining biological values such as 
ungulates and connectivity corridors.
Corridor.Appendices: Appendix 19: Corridor References in the Golden Backcountry Recreation Access Plan (GBRAP)
WildlifeMovement.Information: The plan states that "roads in the lower 12 Mile Creek drainage will be managed for summer motorized recreation (RA3 summer mapping designation) to provide access to both 12 Mile trail heads and for other recreational purposes. A non-motorized corridor (RA1 mapping designation) on the south side of 12 Mile Creek is to manage for wildlife movement."
Network.Information: Ecological networks are not referenced, nor are they managed for.

Plan.Type: Management Plan for a Protected Area or Provincial Park
Plan.Name: Southern Rocky Mountain Management Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan states that "a totally new approach to wildlife connectivity has been developed, through interaction with scientific and technical experts. The emphasis has shifted from definition of wide corridors to utilization of a matrix approach, in which specific ecological elements (e.g. ungulate habitats, grizzly bear avalanche tracks, riparian zones, old growth and mature forest areas, and inoperable forest) are managed in a coordinated manner."

Objective 3.1.2 emphasizes that ecological values like connectivity will not prevent mining activities, as mining approval processes remain prioritized, even in areas designated for habitat protection or wildlife corridors - plan indicates that these activities will be managed with best management practices (including the Mineral Exploration Code) 

Connectivity is considered a key factor when recruiting new OGMAs and MMAs; when establishing/replacing these areas, connectivity is one of the factors to be evaluated.

Under the management intents of trapping, trapping will consider the sensitivity of connectivity corridors.

Connectivity is a management intent/issue of several ecological factors (see appendix 20).

It is established that "Crown land allocations for settlement purposes will be directed away from significant environmental or resource values, such as connectivity corridors, key wildlife habitats, and highcapability agricultural lands or forest lands and areas of high subsurface values or interests."

The plan states that "new or expanded infrastructure will consider wildlife values including the need to maintain connectivity, particularly for grizzly bear."

Access management and private lands are recognized to impact connectivity.

Plan establishes a connectivity section that includes numerous management issues, intents, objectives, benefits, and measures of success (see appendix 20).

Plan references that there will be a Souther Rocky Mountain Advisory Committee (SRMAC) that will monitor the "effectiveness of the connectivity matrix and other conservation objectives."

Connectivity is not defined.
Connectivity.References: Wildlife Act - establishes recreation access and development, including area-based access management measures to maintain connectivity.
Connectivity.Appendices: Appendix 20: Connectivity References in Southern Rocky Mountain Management Plan
Corridor.Information:
Connectivity corridors are referenced within the plan (see connectivity column).

Backcountry river corridors within the plan area are defined as 500 meters on each side of the Wigwam River, 500 meters east of the Bull River, and 500 meters west of the Elk River, until enhanced riparian zone mapping is available. Within these corridors, existing roads and river crossings will be maintained, but new road construction is restricted; new river crossings are limited to two temporary bridges at a time and new or expanded facilities are not permitted within these areas.

Natural movement corridors (low passes) are recognized to support the maintenance of connectivity.

Lack of managment objectives/strategies that specifically refererence ecological corridors.

Corridors are not defined.
Corridor.References: Riparian Management Area Guidebook - plan references this guidebook as a component of the best management practices of managing river corridors within plan area.
WildlifeMovement.Information:
Management intent for wide-ranging carnivores: "maintain movement opportunities for wide-ranging carnivores between the plan area and adjacent watersheds, Montana and Alberta including across Highway 3 ."

Management intent for ungulates: 1) "Increase habitat effectiveness and the opportunity for genetic dispersal [and] seasonal and daily movement... of ungulates at the landscape and stand level," and 2) "enhance habitat effectiveness by: minimizing barriers to movement; implementing recreation access management; and, managing habitat deployment."
Network.Information: Ecological networks are not referenced, nor are they managed for.

Plan.Type: Forest Stewardship Plan (FSP)
Plan.Name: Kootenay Business Area Forest Stewardship Plan
Connectivity.Information:
The plan defines connectivity corridors as a connectivity corridor specified/mapped within the Kootenay Boundary Higher Level Plan Order.

Under objectives 1 (biodiversity emphasis), 2 (old/mature forest), and 5 (grizzly bear habitat and connectivity corridors) in the Kootenay Boundary Higher Level Plan Order, the FSP establishes numerous subsequent management results/strategies (see appendix 21).

"KBHLPO Objective 5 Grizzly Bear Habitat and Connectivity Corridors: Mapping has not been completed for grizzly bear habitat therefore this portion of the objective has not been activated."

For wildlife assessments being conducted, the plan establishes that measures will align with the requirements of regional connectivity corridor mapping.

Connectivity is not defined.
Connectivity.References:
Kootenay Boundary Higher Level Plan Order (KBHLPO) - establishes objectives for connectivity planning (e.g., grizzly bear habitat and connectivity corridors).

FPPR Sec. 48 - under the FPPR, when forest activities occur near classified wetlands or lakes, Wildlife Tree Retention Areas must maintain a travel corridor to key habitat areas, such as OGMAs. If these are not available, the corridor should connect to the oldest adjacent forest stands to support landscape-level connectivity for wildlife.

Rocky Mountain Trench Ecosystem Restoration Program -  FSP requires that if an OGMA assessment identifies the need for primary forest activities to restore or maintain biological values, a prescription must be prepared in alignment with the Rocky Mountain Trench Ecosystem Restoration Program recommendations.
Connectivity.Ref.Diag1: Kootenay Boundary Higher Level Plan Order (KBHLPO)
Connectivity.Ref.Diag3: Rocky Mountain Trench Ecosystem Restoration Program
Connectivity.Appendices: Appendix 21: Connectivity References in the Kootenay Business Area Forest Stewardship Plan (FSP)

The plan references ecological corridors only under the umbrella of connectivity corridors (see connectivity column).

Corridors are not defined.

FPPR Sec. 48 - under the FPPR, when forest activities occur near classified wetlands or lakes, Wildlife Tree Retention Areas must maintain a travel corridor to key habitat areas, such as OGMAs. If these are not available, the corridor should connect to the oldest adjacent forest stands to support landscape-level connectivity for wildlife.

It is stated that "riparian areas... provide ecological connectivity between valley bottoms and high elevations, and also from one valley to another" - plan states that the targets for minimum riparian widths are set both under legal acts (FRPA) and the FSC BC Standard.

Forest Stewardship Council (FSC) - plan references that this act sets targets for riparian widths (loosely related with connectivity)

Biodiversity Guidebook - establishes patch size distributions for NDTs in order to maintain landscape connectivity.

Network.Information:
The plan mentions the Conservation Areas Network (CAN) - a "'coarse-filter' approach to maintaining even poorly understood species and ecosystem functions by ensuring that all distinct habitat types are represented in some form of reserves on the landscape. "

The CAN aims to keep portions of ecosystems unmanaged (no logging, roads, or development) to protect poorly understood ecological functions and species; subsequent targets and strategies established (see appendix 73).
Network.References:
FSC National Forest Stewardship of Canada (2018) - establishes Conservation Area Network (CAN) guidelines.

Aichi Targets - plan references it follows Aichi Target 11 for CANs which states by 2020, at least 17 percent of terrestrial and inland waters are conserved through effectively and equitably managed, ecologically representative and well-connected systems of protected areas and other effective area-based conservation measures and integrated into the wider landscapes.

25 by 2025 - plan references it includes this federal target into CAN planning.

Connectivity.Information:
The park is recognized to provide a extensive connectivity corridor that supports willdife movement for several vulnerable species (e.g., grizzlies, wolverine, and potentially fisher) - mentioned as a part of the park's vision.

"The park includes the headwaters of several major creeks, making it a critical core connectivity area for the movement and genetic dispersion of Grizzly Bears" - plan includes a map showing these core connectivity areas.

It is stated that the high amount of infrastructure in Kaslo Lake area ought to be monitored from a connectivity-habitat perspective.

Established management objective: embrace ecosystem based management; subsequent strategy: collaborate with agencies to maintain wildlife habitat/connectivity (grizzlies, mountain goat, wolverine) outside park, including in protected areas and Special Resource Management Zones (SRMZs).

High priority management strategy: undergo two year grizzly bear movement/habitat suitability project encompassing entire park (using GIS to detail connectivity).

Management strategy: discourage mechanized recreation in the plan area to maintain wildlife connectivity.

There is a consideration in the plan to coordinate a long-term approach with other government agencies that includes connectivity/range management.

Includes comments from public concerning connectivity in fish/wildlife management.

Connectivity and connectivity corridors are not defined.

Connectivity.Pages: pp. 6 in chapter 1, 10-11, 20-21, 28 in chapter 2, 3-8, 14-15,  17-18, 21, 27, 61, 65, 73-75 in chapter 3, pp. 2-4, 9, 11 in the glossary, pp. 4-7, 9-13, 15, 17, 19-21, 23, 26 in Arrow Forest District, pp. 4-14 in the Boundary Forest District, 4, 6, 8-9, 11, 13-16, 19, 21, 25, 30, 32 in the Cranbrook Forest District, pp. 4-8, 10, 12-14, 16-19, 21, 23 in the Golden Timber Supply Area, pp. 4-8, 10-11, 13-14, 16, 18, 20, 22-25 in the Invermere Forest District, pp. 4-5, 7-8, 10, 12, 15-16, 18-19, 22, 24-26, 28, and 30 in the Kootenay Lake Forest District.
Connectivity.Information:
The plan defines connectivity corridors as: "land and water areas with characteristics that make them suitable for 
providing biodiversity linkages among late sucessional ecosystems. Maintenance of connectivity corridors is considered important to long term biodiversity health through the provision of opportunities for genetic, species and ecosystem movement over time."

Connectivity is referenced under the defintion of biodiversity emphasis - "Pursuant to the FPC Biodiversity Guidebook, the relative emphasis that is placed on the maintenance of biodiversity for a particular area (normally a landscape unit), mainly through the application of resource management practices to retain old seral vegetation and provide connectivity linkages within and between ecosystems."

Connectivity is referenced under the definition of FENs - "an area of land or water that serves to maintain or restore 
the natural connectivity within the components of an ecosystem, and between ecosystems."

Connectivity is referenced under the defintion of regional biodiversity benchmarks - "a statement of the desired condition and desired spatial extent, in the Kootenay region, of regionally significant fish streams, caribou species and habitats, ungulate winter range, representative ecosystems and regional connectivity and grizzly bears - as primary indicators of regional biodiversity health."

Connectivity is referenced under the definition of support zones - "areas adjacent to protected areas, parks, wildlife habitat areas, sensitive areas or critical habitats which buffer a marked change in resource management emphasis enabling a gradual impact to environmental values. This is accomplished with strategic biodiversity emphasis allocation, guideline application by environmental value, access management, or connectivity management through the Higher Level Plans Policy and Procedures document."

Timber ERDZs that are intended to balance constraints on timber harvesating resulting from guidelines for sensitive environmental and social values are stated to have an absence of regionally significant conservation values, such as regional biodiversity corridors.

Regarding commerical tourism, established management direction that: "Planning and resource allocation decisions should regard commercial recreation as a valid and appropriate use of Crown land, subject to conformance with legislation and policy"; subsequent strategy: Substantial proposals to allocate tenure or title to Crown land for commercial recreation will be referred to affected agencies and evaluated with consideration to specified issues, including regional biodiversity connectivity corridors.

Regarding settlement, utility, and communication uses of Crown land, Established management direction: recognize environmental conservation and other land use and resource management objectives when making decisions on the disposition of Crown land for settlement and other purposes; subsequent strategy: proposals for allocating Crown land for settlement will be reviewed with other agencies to avoid areas of regional significance, such as biodiversity connectivity corridors. A coordinated approach to siting utility and transportation corridors will be promoted within biodiversity connectivity corridors to minimize linear barriers to ecological values.

Regarding general ecosystem health, established management direction: "maintain the quality, integrity, and connectivity of grassland habitats to support associated red- and blue-listed species"; subsequent strategy: Natural grassland communities will be restored and maintained, and grazing management regimes will incorporate requirements for sensitive wildlife habitat areas identified in approved range and forest development plans.

Regarding terrestrial ecosystem health, established management direction: "Maintain the regional diversity 
and a suitable abundance of native terrestrial species of plants and animals, and the ecosystems upon which they depend"; subsequent strategies: 1) develop and maintain a regional network of connectivity corridorsand other habitat features to support the distribution of species, populations, and genetic material, 2) prevent or minimize fragmentation of lands within regional connectivity corridors through connectivity guidelines, 3) prioritize locating old growth management areas within or near connectivity corridors to maintain interior forest habitat conditions, and 4) manage corridors to meet biodiversity emphasis levels and provide interim connectivity until landscape unit objectives are finalized.

Regarding rangeland ecosystems; established management direction: "maintain highly diverse and contiguous rangelands"; subsequent strategy: rangeland ecosystem management in regional connectivity corridors will occur according to specfied Seral Stage Guidelines (see appendix 112).

As a guideline intent of the plan, it is stated that (per the Biodiversity Guidebook) that the habitat needs of most forest and range organisms can be provided for by "maintaining connectivity of ecosystems in such a manner as to ensure the continued dispersal and movement of forest- and range-dwelling organisms  across the landscape"

Listed as a general management approach of mature plus old and old seral targets - in High and Intermediate Emphasis interim Landscape Units with seral deficits, design a recruitment strategy to identify OGMAs and mature areas that quickly meet seral targets while optimizing connectivity, representation, and interior conditions. Generally, avoid harvesting old seral stands below seral targets, except in rare cases where small harvests enable large patch recruitment elsewhere.

There is an included section outlining general managment intent, approaches, and guidelines for regional connectivity (see appendix 113).

Regarding grizzly bear management guidelines, 1) use regional connectivity corridors to provide dispersal corridors between areas of population concentrations (such as parks). 2) forest managment direction that riparian areas at the base of avalanche tracks should be managed to retain connectivity to upper elevations, and 3) access management will prioritize areas where grizzly bear habitat overlaps with regional connectivity corridors until the Grizzly Bear Conservation Strategy defines specific areas and access priorities.

Regarding ungulate winter range management guidelines, there is a general management approach that "consistent with biophysical attributes, forest habitat cover should be arranged to maintain the most suitable connectivity, interior forest conditions and edge attributes."

Regarding elk, mule deer, whitetail deer, and moose - established habitat management objective to maintain connectivity requirements and habitat values.

In a chart containing Stand-level management objectives and silvicultural strategies for winter habitat, there is a management objective to maintain stand level connectivity in the EW-ICH, EW-ESSF, and LW-ESSF zones; subsequent strategies: 1) avoid excessive physical obstructions (e.g., windthrown slash, many down trees), and 2) thorough slashing of logging debris, especially if in large quantity - included rationale that caribou caribou seems to prefer 
areas where movement is not obstructed by debris or vegetation.

It is included that connectivity corridors will be acknowledged in arranging Restoration operations across the landscape - "In the absence of Landscape Unit Plan objectives, planning for individual entries (harvest or restoration) should examine the surrounding 250-2500 ha area to ensure connectivity and interaction with old growth, ungulate winter range and recreation guidelines are achieved."

The plan states that most regional connectivity corridors are excluded from the Timber Enhanced Resource Development Zone (ERDZ-T) for timber management, ensuring that these areas are not subject to intensive silvicultural activities.

Within the Crown Land for Settlement Uses guidelines, the plan recognizes that human settlement overlaps with critical low elevation wildlife habitats and regional connectivity corridors since most settlement occurs in low elevation valleys within the region - "efforts will also be made to avoid or accommodate the requirements for regionally significant biodiversity connectivity corridors (see section 3.2) and wildlife habitats."

Regarding park management, there is an established management direction to ensure protection of key species and 
their habitats; subsequent strategy that "BC Parks will work with other agencies to ensure connectivity of wildlife habitat between the park and surrounding area."

In the Arrow Forest District, there is a listed management objective to maintain regional connectivity corridors for regionally significant species (e.g., grizzly bear, caribou) in the Faith and Peter Creeks, South Salmo River and Lost Creek, Hutchison, Slocan Valley, Headwaters of Eagle Creek, Headwaters Cortiana and Galloping Creeks, the Pinnacles, Bear Lake, Lardeau Range, Hill and Mackenzie Creeks, Lower Columbia, Kootenay Pend d'Oreille and Salmo Rivers, Winlaw Creek, Upper Carpenter, Silverton, Enterprise, and Springer Creeks, Lower Arrow Lake, Upper Arrow Lake, and Incomappleux River RMZs; subsequent strategy: "apply the connectivity guidelines within the 
regional connectivity corridor as indicated in Chapter 3, section 3.3" (see appendix 113).

In the Boundary Forest District, there is a listed management objective to maintain regional connectivity corridors for regionally significant species (e.g., grizzly bear, caribou) in the Gilpin Grasslands, Snowball Creek, West Side of Christina Lake, Texas Creek, Galloping Mountain and Headwaters of Burrell Creek, Goatskin Creek, Kettle-Granby, Kettle River, and Christina Lake/Sutherland Creek RMZs; subsequent strategy: "apply the connectivity guidelines within the regional connectivity corridor as indicated in Chapter 3, section 3.3" (see appendix 113).

In the Cranbrook Forest District, there is a listed management objective to maintain regional connectivity corridors for regionally significant species (e.g., grizzly bear, caribou) in the Upper Elk Valley - West Side, North Galbraith, Pickering Hills, Flathead River Corridor, Upper Wigwam River, Gilnockie, Upper Meachen Creek, Premier Ridge, Diorite Creek-Premier Face, Bull River, Limestone Range, Sage/Commerce Creeks, Rocky Mountain Trench, Southern Purcell Mountains, and Elk Valley East RMZs; subsequent strategy: "apply the connectivity guidelines within the regional connectivity corridor as indicated in Chapter 3, section 3.3" (see appendix 113) - and only for Rocky Mountain Trench - "human settlement plans, including Official Community Plans, to address and reflect regional connectivity."

For the Golden Timber Supply Area, there is a listed management objective to maintain regional connectivity corridors for regionally significant species (e.g., grizzly bear, caribou) in the Upper Wood River, Windy Creek, Mt. Sir Sandford and the Adamants, Canyon Creek, Moberly Marshes, Moose Creek, Encampment Creek and Wood Arm, Clemenceau Icefields; Kinbasket, Sullivan and Bush Rivers, Westside of Kinbasket Lake and Blackwater Ridge, Bachelor Creek, Gold River, Ventego Creek, Valentciennes, Upper Bluewater and Blaeberry, Dogtooth Range, Westbench, Golden - Donald, Northbench, Kicking Horse, and Beaverfoot and Kootenay Rivers RMZs; subsequent strategy: "apply the connectivity guidelines within the regional connectivity corridor as indicated in Chapter 3, section 3.3" (see appendix 113).

For the Invermere Forest District, there is a listed management objective to maintain regional connectivity corridors for regionally significant species (e.g., grizzly bear, caribou) in the Upper Spillamacheen, Columbia River Wetlands, Albert River, North White River, Kotsats Creek, Columbia Lake (East Side), Jumbo-Upper Horsethief, Fir Mountain (North Side), Mid-Findlay - Purcell, Buhl - Skookumchuck, Premier Ridge, Diorite Creek-Premier Face, East Kootenay Trench, Steamboat-Bugaboo, Kootenay - White River, and Lower Dutch - Lavington Creek RMZs; subsequent strategy: "apply the connectivity guidelines within the regional connectivity corridor as indicated in Chapter 3, section 3.3" (see appendix 113) - this strategy is replace with "apply high emphasis biodiversity, as per the FPC, as indicated in Chapter 3, section 3.2" for the Mid-Findlay - Purcell RMZ, and is paired with the strategy of "human settlement plans, including Official Community Plans, to address and reflect regional connectivity" for the Premier Ridge, Diorite Creek-Premier Face and East Kootenay Trench RMZs.

There is a listed managment objective for the Kootenay - White River RMZ to "ensure that rangeland attributes 
within the regional connectivity corridors and high elevation grasslands in the Emily Creek, Home Basin, Marmalade Creek, Mary Anne Creek, Mutton Creek/Nine-Mile Creek/Mount Glenn, Van Nostrand Range and White Knight Peak areas are maintained or enhanced"; subsequent strategy: "Develop and implement an access management plan for high elevation grasslands in accordance with the Access Management guidelines (Chapter 3, section 3.12)" (see appendix 114).

For the Kootenay Lake Forest District, there is a listed management objective to maintain regional connectivity corridors for regionally significant species (e.g., grizzly bear, caribou) in the Priest River and Summit Creek Headwaters, Creston Valley Wildlife Management Area, Arrow Creek Watershed, Mallandaine, Kamma and Upper Kid Creeks, Midge, North and West Arms of Kootenay Lake, Lyle and Whitewater Creeks, Lardeau, Cooper and Meadow Creeks, Upper Glacier Creek, Trout Lake, Kootenay, Goat and Moyie Rivers, Rover and Fortynine Creeks, Upper Sproule and Grohman CreeksKootenay Lake, East Side, Davis, Lostledge and Schroeder Creeks, and Duncan River RMZs; subsequent strategy: "apply the connectivity guidelines within the regional connectivity corridor as indicated in Chapter 3, section 3.3" (see appendix 113).

The plan includes rationales for BEO assignments of various landscape units in the Kootenay Lake, Invermere, Golden, Cranbrook, Boundary, and Arrow that describes connectivity values.
